title: session-consistent-lifecycle-gatekeeper-for-experiments-and-canaries
preview:
Context and motivation:
This PR introduces the 'Lifecycle Gatekeeper', a coordinator that unifies canary deploys, A/B experiments, shadow seeding, and automated rollback workflows while preserving session consistency for stateful flows (chat sessions, transactions, etc.). Prod teams requested an out-of-the-box mechanism that: 1) keeps user sessions routed to stable variants during phased rollouts, 2) applies SLO/QA gates driven by telemetry windows, and 3) supports staged shadow traffic with backpressure and replay for testing.

Design summary:
- Lease-based session bonding: the gatekeeper issues short-lived leases tied to session identifiers (cookie/session_id). During ramp phases, existing sessions remain bound to the pre-rollout variant while new sessions may be admitted to target variants per policy. Leases survive process restarts and are persisted in the existing session-store abstraction.
- Telemetry-synced gates: gates evaluate sliding-window metrics (latency, error-rate, hallucination-rate) from the telemetry pipeline. Policies are declarative (promote_if, rollback_if) and accept multiple metric predicates with boolean logic.
- Shadow orchestration with backpressure: we added a staged shadow lane that applies probabilistic sampling, rate-limited replay of shadow responses to a dry-run eval pipeline, and adaptive backpressure if downstream evaluators fall behind. This reduces blast radius when enabling shadow seeding at scale.
- Auto-rollback and recovery: a configurable 'rollback_advisor' synthesizes gate signals and decides whether to auto-backout a roll based on confidence heuristics and trend detection. It supports dry-run recommendations and an automated rollback path if thresholds are exceeded.
- Compatibility & config: new rollout config keys are optional. The default behavior mirrors current rollouts unless 'session_consistency: true' is set. Admins can opt into 'lease_ttl', 'telemetry_window', 'shadow_sampling', and 'auto_rollback_policy'.

Why this is different from existing controllers:
Most previous controllers focused on traffic fractions or cohort anchoring without explicit session affinity or integrated shadow backpressure. This Gatekeeper ties session guarantees and telemetry-driven decisions together so customer-facing stateful features can safely rollout model changes.

Implementation notes (high level):
- services/orchestrator/gatekeeper.go: new coordinator, lease lifecycle, policy evaluator, rollback_advisor hooks.
- pkg/session/lease_store.go: session lease persistence with adapters for redis and the existing postgres-backed session table.
- pkg/telemetry/gates.go: predicate compiler and sliding window aggregator (uses existing meter ingestion topics).
- controllers/rollout/crd.yml: schema updates for the optional lifecycle fields (validated by the CRD conversion webhook).
- e2e tests: integration tests simulate 10k sessions with a staged ramp and assert session stickiness, correct gate behavior, and rollback paths.

Rollout plan:
- Canary in staging using a Director-managed deployment (dry-run mode) for 48h.
- Gradual production ramp: enable session_consistency for a single low-traffic tenant; observe telemetry and smoke tests for 72h.
- If stable, flip default flag for new rollouts. A migration job exists to backfill leases for long-lived sessions if teams choose to opt-in.

Checklist (completed):
- [x] Design doc reviewed by product and SRE (ENG-4210).
- [x] Unit and integration tests added.
- [x] Backwards-compatible config and CRD migration path.
- [x] Basic load-tested shadow backpressure under 2x normal traffic.
- [x] Canary in staging performed.

Open items (follow-ups):
- Add quantile-aware predicates to telemetry gates (follow-up ticket assigned).
- UI for rollout visualization (PM scope).

title: Hybrid comm/compute: staggered NCCL streams + dynamic kernel placement for tensor-parallel workloads
preview:
Motivation: For TP models we observed tail latency spikes when large collective operations serialized across the default NCCL stream and blocked compute kernels on subsequent micro-batches. This PR implements a hybrid comm/compute execution strategy: staggered NCCL comm streams per tensor-parallel group, opportunistic kernel placement on secondary compute streams, and a lightweight dynamic placement heuristic that routes small-kernel work to a dedicated low-latency stream to maximize overlap without increasing memory pressure.

Summary of changes: implemented a per-TP-group stream allocator that staggers all-reduce/all-gather launches across NCCL streams (comm_stream_0..N) to reduce contention; added a kernel placement layer in the runtime scheduler that classifies kernels by inferred latency (fast vs slow) and assigns them to either the main compute stream or a fast-path microbatch stream; introduced a KV-cache prefetch gate that issues early prefetches on a priority stream when sequence prefix hits are predicted; added guards for CUDA Graph capture compatibility and a deterministic fallback for constrained environments.

Design notes and rationale: staggering is conservative by default (stride = 2) and can be tuned per-deployment via runtime config (SERVER_TP_COMM_STRIDE). The placement heuristic uses a small sampled histogram of kernel durations (collected during warmup) and a tunable threshold; thresholds are persisted in the worker cache and aged out after config changes. We avoid aggressive fusion for very large kernels because it increases register pressure and can reduce overall throughput on A100-like hardware. The KV prefetch gate reduces latency for long context prefix hits by pre-warming the attention KV buffers on the priority stream while the previous micro-batch tokens finish compute on the main stream.

Testing and benchmarks: microbench harness updated under perf/bench/tp to include a staggered-comm profile. Results on a 16xA100-80GB cluster (tensor-parallel 8 across 2 GPUs per node) show: 9-14% single-request p99 latency reduction for 1-4 token continuations, and 8-12% end-to-end throughput improvement for large-batch streaming workloads when using the default stride=2. Memory overhead observed ~1.7% due to additional stream state and a small histogram buffer. Bench scripts and raw data attached in the PR (perf/bench/results/pr-24672/).

Files touched: runtime scheduler, low-level NCCL wrapper, kernel metadata table, kvcache prefetch codepath, perf harness and tests, docs note in docs/perf/tp-comm-tuning.md.

Migration and rollout: feature guarded behind runtime flag tp.hybrid_comm.enabled=false by default on main. Canary rollout plan: enable on dedicated performance canaries (redwood/perf-canary) for 72h, monitor token-level latency traces and p99 regressions. Automatic rollback if p99 increases by >3% or if GPU OOMs are observed.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for stream allocator and placement heuristic - [x] Integration test (simulated multi-GPU TP in CI) - [x] Perf harness and baseline comparison - [x] Docs and runtime config entries - [x] Canary/rollback plan

Related work: builds on prior NCCL tuning efforts (see ENG-1684 and prior PRs) but differs by introducing dynamic kernel placement and KV prefetch gating. Addresses customer case SUP-455 that reported tail latency on TP configs.

Notes: kept the changes small and feature-flagged to reduce blast radius. This PR intentionally does not enable CUDA Graph capture when the hybrid comm path is active; that follow-up will require graph replay orchestration and is tracked as ENG-1899.

title: introduce-cost-caddy-prefill-heuristics-and-quant-fallbacks
preview:
Motivation: Many tenants ship with conservative prefill and quantization defaults which are safe but expensive. This PR introduces 'Cost Caddy', a lightweight prefill-heuristics controller that applies tenant-aware default batching/caching presets and a safe quantization fallback policy to reduce unit token cost while preserving quality SLOs. Goals: 1) Provide deterministic, explainable defaults for prefill and micro-batching tuned to observed traffic affinity. 2) Add a safety-first quantization fallback so tenants automatically degrade to a compatible, higher-precision variant only when quality or latency regressions are detected. 3) Surface prefilling telemetry so ops teams can validate cost/latency tradeoffs. Summary of changes:
- New runtime controller "cost_caddy" that computes an allocation hint (micro-batch shape, prefill window, cache TTL) per tenant based on short-term traffic profile and tenant tier.
- A quant-fallback policy engine that attaches a graded fallback chain to model variants (e.g. q4->q5->fp16) with configurable thresholds for quality drift and token-latency.
- Telemetry tags and histograms for prefill hit-rate, prefilling cost delta, and fallback triggers; added debug traces for the decision path.
- CLI tool (redwood cli prefill-sim) to dry-run candidate defaults using replayed request traces.
- Defaults file under config/defaults/cost_caddy.yaml with conservative seed profiles and human-readable rationale. Rollout plan: Start as an opt-in feature under feature flag redwood.prefill.cost_caddy; run for 3 tenants in staging and 10 low-traffic tenants in canary for one week; collect prefilling telemetry and run the automated quality regressions. On successful validation, enable for all free-tier tenants to capture early cost gains before expanding to enterprise tenants with opt-out. Testing: unit tests for the decision logic, integration test that validates quant-fallback invocation on simulated degradation, load test showing median token-cost reduction of 11% on a representative small-batch workload, and cli replay verifications. Backwards compatibility: all defaults are applied only when a tenant has not set explicit overrides; quant-fallback is non-destructive and keeps the original model pinned unless thresholds are crossed. Related work: references ENG-2389 (prefill telemetry schema) and ENG-2401 (quant fallback design doc).

title: hybrid-staged-canary-controller-with-statistical-gates-and-fallthrough-routing
preview: Motivation: enable safer multi-stage experiments and automated rollback by introducing a hybrid staged canary controller that combines time/traffic phasing, Bayesian statistical gates, and deterministic fallthrough routing. This PR implements a controller that can run A/B tests, shadow trials, and progressive canaries using a configurable stability window, significance threshold, and quarantine queue. Key features: 1) BayesianSignificanceEngine (configurable priors) to decide pass/fail for metric families; 2) HealthSnapshot store that captures per-route histograms and model KV cache hit rates at sample intervals; 3) WeightDecaySchedule to gradually reduce canary traffic on persistent regressions before triggering full rollback; 4) FallthroughRouting that routes traffic to a fallback model variant instantly on hard failures while preserving request affinity; 5) QuarantineQueue and automated re-evaluation that isolates problematic model variants and automatically schedules a re-test after a cool-down with adjusted batch/quant settings; 6) DriftDetector to abort experiments when input distribution divergence exceeds threshold; 7) Integration hooks for Redwood Optimize to emit suggested quantization/batching profiles for canaries. Checklist: - [x] controller implementation and reconciliation loop - [x] Bayesian engine + unit tests - [x] health snapshot persistence and API - [x] fallthrough routing and affinity preservation - [x] quarantine queue and automated re-test job - [x] shadow traffic passthrough mode - [x] docs and runbook updates - [x] e2e smoke test in perf-canary harness How to test locally: run ./hack/dev-canary with two local model variants, set linear key ENG-9123 as test target, verify that induced regression (synthetic label noise) triggers weight decay then quarantine and eventual rollback. Backward compatibility: defaults preserve previous canary semantics if hybrid_mode=false. Migration: adds CRD fields canary.strategy and canary.statsEngine but controller tolerates absent fields. Related: ENG-9123 for rollout design, ENG-9188 for metric extraction, SUP-447 incident notes. Impact: improves safety and reduces noisy manual rollbacks; expected to reduce manual interventions by ~40% for staged rollouts (internal estimate).

title: tokenized-fairness-scaler-and-util-gap-reporter
preview:
Context and motivation: customers on Dedicated have asked for better fairness when bursts co-exist with steady throughput tenants. Existing burst pools and reservations ensure throughput but can starve latency-sensitive tenants during microbursts. This PR implements a tokenized fairness scaler: a lightweight token accounting layer that mints per-tenant 'burst tokens' proportional to committed throughput and dynamically redistributes spare tokens based on queue-latency buckets.

Design summary: the scaler observes per-tenant request queue latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) and token consumption rate. When a tenant's p95 exceeds its latency target and tokens are available in the cluster pool, the allocator grants ephemeral tokens that raise the tenant's concurrency cap for short windows (configurable TTL). Tokens are reclaimed proactively when latency recovers or when they expire. Token minting respects tenant share weights and a soft floor so smaller tenants cannot be starved.

Utilization Gap Reporter: a new telemetry pipeline sampler that computes 'util-gap' = committed_capacity - observed_effective_throughput over rolling windows. Significant positive gap (>X%) indicates underutilized reserved capacity; negative gap suggests chronic oversubscription and triggers alerts. The reporter aggregates per-tenant and per-cluster signals and emits events to the Console and to the autoscaler's feedback loop.

Key behaviors and safety: - Autoscaler only grants tokens if cluster headroom >= headroom_threshold config. - Tokens are time-limited (default 30s) and double-checked before increasing concurrency to avoid drift. - Manual overrides: operators can pin token budgets per-tenant via the Dedicated Console. - Feature gated: flag 'tokenized_fair_scaler_v1' controls rollout.

Files of note: see changed files list below for implementation and tests.

Testing and validation: - Unit tests for token accounting, TTL expiry, and reclaim logic. - Integration tests with synthetic traffic generator that simulates bursty and steady tenants and validates latency SLOs improve without increasing aggregate CPU/Tensor utilization. - Canary plan: deploy to a single Dedicated cluster with low-risk tenants (internal sandbox), monitor util-gap and latency deltas for 48h, then expand to two clusters before full rollout.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked (ENG-1842 tokenized-fairness-scaler) - [x] Unit & integration tests - [x] Telemetry for util-gap metrics - [x] Console toggles and tenant budget API - [x] Feature flag behind canary - [x] Safety tests and chaos run

Related: references existing autoscaler work (ENG-1532) but intentionally keeps runtime changes minimal to reduce risk.

title: Rate-limited reservation stitcher and FairBank tenancy allocator
preview:
Context: Dedicated capacity is often fragmented by many small reservations and short-lived allocations. This fragmentation reduces effective utilization and drives unnecessary autoscaler churn. Previous approaches (aggressive backfilling and larger warm pools) either increased cost or exposed tenants to noisy neighbors.

This PR implements two coordinated pieces: 1) a rate-limited 'reservation stitcher' that periodically merges adjacent, compatible reservations into larger contiguous reservation blocks to reduce fragmentation, and 2) a new tenancy allocator named 'FairBank' that uses weighted proportional allocation with soft budget banking to preserve per-tenant throughput guarantees while improving global packing efficiency.

Key design points: the stitcher only operates on reservations that are compatible by model family, region, and isolation constraints; it applies a token-bucket rate limiter to stitch operations to avoid allocation storms during recovery windows; every stitch is recorded as an atomic plan so it can be safely rolled back on failure. FairBank maintains per-tenant debt/balance counters and enforces a soft spend floor so high-usage tenants cannot immediately consume freshly stitched capacity; the allocator includes a configurable rebalancing window and cooldown to prevent oscillation.

Telemetry and knobs: added metrics stitcher_ops_total, stitch_reverts_total, stitched_capacity_ratio, fairbank_balance_gauge, and fairness_index_p99. Config flags: feature.gate.reservation_stitcher (opt-in), stitcher.rate_limit_qps, fairbank.rebalance_window_seconds, allocator.cooldown_seconds. Default gates off for release to enable staged rollouts.

Testing & results: unit tests for stitch plan generation and rollback; race tests for concurrent stitch attempts; integration-scale job that simulates 150 tenants with realistic reservation churn. Results on benchmark lab: 6-12% utilization gain on mixed workloads, tail latency unchanged within 1-2% for tenant-controlled SLOs.

Migration & rollout: ship behind gate; start with stitcher.rate_limit_qps=0 (disabled), enable in canary region with conservative qps, monitor stitched_capacity_ratio and stitch_reverts_total. If reverts spike, reduce qps or disable. Fallback path: allocator can run in legacy compatibility mode by setting fairbank.mode=legacy.

Why this is different: rather than aggressive prewarming or larger warm pools, stitching reclaims fragmented capacity in-situ with minimal cross-tenant disturbance, and FairBank provides a budget-aware allocation scheme that prevents short-term allocation storms from consuming newly stitched resources.

Related: ENG-1429 (fragmentation investigation), PM-387 (cost-efficiency objective), SUP-721 (customer report about cold starts during commit storms).

Related: ENG-1429 (fragmentation investigation), PM-387 (cost-efficiency objective), SUP-721 (customer report about cold starts during commit storms).

title: add difficulty-calibrator-and-heuristic-resampler-ci-hook
preview: Motivation: CI quality gates have been vulnerable to false positives/negatives when prompt sets shift subtly in difficulty (length, novelty vs. anchor set, generation entropy). This PR introduces a difficulty-calibrator component that scores prompts by a composite difficulty metric and a heuristic resampler used both during local eval runs and as a CI hook to detect distributional shifts. What changed: 1) eval_harness/difficulty/calibrator.py: new module that computes a composite difficulty score per prompt (token-length-normalized surprisal proxy from a lightweight LM scoring pass, embedding distance to anchored prompt centroids, and response-model entropy estimate). 2) eval_harness/ci/hooks/difficulty_gate.py: CI hook that compares the baseline difficulty distribution (stored as a short histogram blob in CI artifacts) to the current run using a K-S style test plus a shift-aware regression control. 3) eval_harness/samplers/heuristic_resampler.py: on-disk reweighting/resampling strategy to rebalance evaluation batches by difficulty quintiles and optionally prioritize historically unstable prompts. 4) tests/*: unit tests and small integration smoke tests. 5) docs/eval/difficulty_calibrator.md: design doc and recommended thresholds for CI. Design notes / how it works: - Composite difficulty score is a weighted sum (configurable) of:  a) normalized surprisal estimate (fast model-forward on a small ngram LM checkpoint)  b) semantic novelty (embedding cosine distance to nearest 50-anchor prompts)  c) response entropy proxy derived from prewired model-forward token logits for a short rollout. - The CI hook computes two signals: (A) distributional shift score (K-S on difficulty scalar) and (B) difficulty-conditioned regression delta: prompts are bucketed into quintiles and the primary quality metric (e.g., F1/ROUGE/semantic-sim) is compared per-bin to baseline; the hook fails only if either (1) the overall shift exceeds a tunable threshold AND the difficulty-conditioned per-bin regression exceeds per-bin thresholds OR (2) there is a significant regression concentrated in a bin with low sample count that cannot be explained by sampling variance. - The heuristic resampler reweights the eval queue to match a target difficulty distribution (default: baseline quintile proportions) and includes an 'attenuate-hotspots' option to avoid repeatedly exposing the same unstable prompts. Testing & CI: - Added deterministic-seed tests for calibrator computations and a small simulated-run integration test that exercises the gate with contrived baseline/current distributions. - CI initially failed on test_rebalance_sample due to non-determinism in the random seed used by the sampling smoke test; fixed by pinning RNG in the test and re-running. - Full pipeline (unit + smoke + lint) passed on rerun. Rollout plan: - Enabled as opt-in in CI for 1 week (flag: ci.enable_difficulty_gate=false by default). After observing false positive rate <1% on main branch historical windows and one week of canary on the release/* lanes, flip to opt-out. - Ops/Oncall runbook section added in docs describing how to interpret per-bin regressions and how to backfill baseline histograms. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is no-op unless baseline artifact present and ci flag enabled; no breaking changes to public APIs.

title: Contextual weighted sample allocator and gradual restrictor for stable eval gating
preview:
Motivation: CI evals have been noisy for lower-volume prompt cohorts. Teams are getting frequent false positives when a small cohort flips. This PR implements a contextual weighted sample allocator that allocates probe budget across prompt sets using a combination of: (1) prompt-level importance (manually tagged or derived from production hit rates), (2) recent drift score (rolling 7-day), and (3) semantic novelty (via embedding distance).

Design: The allocator produces proportional sample quotas per cohort and emits a per-run sampling plan. A complementary GradualRestrictor replaces the previous immediate hard-blocking CI hook: instead of a binary fail, we compute a severity score and apply a staged response (soft-fail annotations, delayed blocking windows, and adaptive replays). This reduces churn while preserving safety for true regressions.

What changed (high level):
- New allocator: eval_harness/sampler/contextual_allocator.py (core logic, pluggable backends)
- Gradual restrictor: eval_harness/ci/gradual_restrictor.py (CI hook adapter)
- Scheduler integration: eval_harness/orchestrator/sampling_scheduler.py (wiring + metrics)
- Tests: tests/samplers/test_contextual_allocator.py, tests/ci/test_gradual_restrictor.py
- Docs: docs/evals/sampling.md (usage + config knobs)

Compatibility and config: The allocator is off by default. Config keys under eval.sampling.contextual_enabled=true to enable. Backwards-compatible: if not enabled the legacy uniform sampler is used. GradualRestrictor is configurable via eval.ci.restrictor.mode with options [off, soft, gradual, strict].

Test plan: unit tests added with deterministic seeds, synthetic drift traces, and an integration smoke test in CI that runs a small sampling plan. Benchmarked on staging with existing prompt suites — observed 41% reduction in transient CI failures for low-volume cohorts while maintaining high sensitivity for seeded regressions.

Checklist:
- [x] Unit tests
- [x] Integration smoke
- [x] Docs
- [x] Perf profiling (staging)
- [x] Feature flag gated and default-off in prod

Related: ENG-2261 (sampling strategy), ENG-2278 (ci-stability initiatives).

title: add-inference-health-probe-and-sampled-kernel-queue-explorer
preview: Adds a lightweight inference health probe and a sampled kernel-queue explorer to improve incident triage and long-tail cost attribution. Motivation: recurring short-lived kernel queue stalls were noisy in SLO pages and made it hard to trace whether slow tails were caused by model-level token spikes, kernel contention, or tenant-induced backpressure. This PR introduces: 1) inference_health_probe: periodic synthetic pings that exercise a small, deterministic token path and emit a health metric (inference_probe_latency_ms) plus an opaque probe_id for trace linking; 2) sampled_kernel_queue_explorer: a new trace sampler that, when a probe or real invocation exceeds a kernel-queue latency threshold, captures a compact kernel-queue snapshot and stores a compacted span with queue_depth, head_kernel_id, and estimated_token_cost_hint; 3) dashboard panels: a compact 'Probe Pulse' timeseries, kernel-queue heatmap (bucketed by queue depth and model shard), and a 'Probe vs Real' latency scatter for quick triage; 4) runbook/playbook: an Observability Surge Playbook page with automated escalation steps and quick links to tenant-level cost and KV cache hit panels. Implementation notes: probe logic is opt-in per deployment via config.inference.probe.enabled (false by default) and probes are low-rate by design (0.1 rps per region) to avoid billing noise; the sampled kernel snapshots are stored in the observability compact store with a capped reservoir to limit cardinality. Backwards compatibility: no changes to public API surfaces. Checklist: [x] add probe runtime hooks, [x] add metric and span schema, [x] add dashboard panels, [x] add playbook and runbook links, [x] update docs and config reference, [x] add unit tests and e2e sampler smoke test. Related: ENG-6123 for incident RCA and OBS-47 for dashboard request. Expected rollout: feature gated for 2 weeks, enable for staging then incrementally to prod with guardrail on stored snapshot volume.

title: Topology-aware peer balancing, async kernel dispatch, and KV-cache coalescing
preview: Motivation: Multi-GPU tensor-parallel workloads suffer from suboptimal collective placement and small-KV churn when requests are micro-batched across heterogeneous racks. This PR implements three coordinated runtime optimizations to improve throughput and tail latency: (1) topology-aware peer balancing: build NVLink/NIC-aware ring/graph membership using a lightweight port-speed probe so collectives prefer intra-node/NVLink lanes and minimize cross-node hops, and dynamically rebalance peer pairs when per-step bandwidth estimates indicate hotlinks; (2) async kernel dispatch: move per-layer kernel launch to a dispatch thread pair that annotates CUDA streams with priority and orders kernels for overlap with NCCL comms, and add a non-blocking enqueue-locality check so compute work can start as soon as required dependencies are queued; (3) KV-cache coalescing at the request router: group concurrent small KV reads into coalesced bulk fetches and fold redundant cache population work to reduce memory traffic and small-launch overheads. Design notes: peer selection runs at model load and is re-evaluated when topology events are seen (PCIe/NVLink health changes, new GPUs added). Peer balancing uses a lightweight EWMA of measured pairwise bandwidths to pick an alternating pairing sequence for each micro-batch window. Async dispatch uses two prioritized CUDA streams per device (compute_high, compute_low) plus a comm stream; we rely on CUDA stream priorities instead of artificial host sleeps which reduces scheduling jitter. KV coalescer is conservatively enabled behind the feature flag tp_topo_pb and has a max-coalesce window (configurable) to avoid added latency for single-request critical paths. Implementation summary: runtime/scheduler: add TopologyProbe and PeerBalancer modules, extend DeviceResources to expose stream priorities; runtime/comm: new TopoRingBuilder that prefers NVLink edges, updates NCCL unique IDs to include pairing hints; runtime/dispatch: KernelDispatcher refactor, introduce DispatchFence for dependency tracking and switch to cudaLaunchKernelAsync for fused kernels; router/kv_cache: RequestCoalescer layer with in-process batching and de-duplication. Compatibility and safety: fallbacks to previous ring builder when topology probe fails or when running on unsupported drivers; coalescer has a conservative timeout and a per-model opt-out. Tests and validation: microbenchmarks using benchmark-lab show 18-25% throughput improvement on 8xA100 NVLink topology for half-precision decode workloads with 2048 seq; 95th percentile tail latency reduced by ~14-20% for 8k token streams when KV coalescing is enabled. Perf canary across 3 dedicated clusters showed stable error rates and no observable degradation in output quality. Rollout plan: feature-flagged in main, staged canary to dedicated-qa, then incremental ramp to production Dedicated pools over 72 hours with monitoring on NCCL error counters, GPU PCIe errors, end-to-end latency SLOs. Observability: emit metrics peer_balance/pair_changes, topo_probe/latency_ms, kvcoalesce/coalesce_rate, dispatcher/stream_stall_ms, and add tracing spans for coalescer windows. Documentation: updated runtime/README and redwood-docs/perf for recommended config knobs. CI and perf runs: included unit tests for peer balancer invariants and integration tests in perf-harness. This change is primarily internal runtime improvements; no user API changes expected.

title: stabilize-stream-framing-and-introduce-retry-hint-headers-for-tool-calls
preview: Motivation: Production customers observed intermittent stream desynchronization for tool/function-call flows and inconsistent client retry behavior across OpenAI-compatible adapters. This PR stabilizes server->client stream framing for tool calls by adding explicit lifecycle frames and improves retry semantics by introducing a machine-friendly Retry-Hint header plus an Idempotency-Timestamp header to disambiguate partial successes. The change set: 1) Stream framing: add two lightweight frame types ("heartbeat" and "finalize") to the streaming protocol used for function/tool outputs so clients have deterministic markers for request liveness and end-of-output; this fixes race conditions where clients treated reconnects as duplicates. 2) Retry hints: add Retry-Hint header (JSON-safe string with recommended backoff window and idempotency-sensitivity tag) emitted on 429/503/504 errors and in preflight responses, and formalize parsing rules to accept both delta-seconds and HTTP-date formats in Retry-After. 3) Idempotency: introduce an optional Idempotency-Timestamp header along with the existing Idempotency-Key; the timestamp allows servers and clients to reason about partial writes and safe retries when the adapter is uncertain about delivery, documented migration guidance included. 4) Compatibility layer: the OpenAI compatibility shim maps legacy X-RateLimit-* and Retry-After variants into the new Retry-Hint envelope and preserves previous string-based 429 messages but adds a structured error.code field to align with platform error taxonomy. 5) Tests & docs: end-to-end streaming tests added for reconnect+resume, unit tests for header parsing (HTTP-date and seconds), and API docs updated with examples and a client migration checklist. Backwards compatibility: default behavior remains unchanged for clients that ignore the new headers; streaming framing adds optional frames that older clients will ignore safely. Rollout: feature-flagged on account tier; gradual ramp and telemetry added to monitor false positives in duplicate detection. Impact: reduces client-observed duplicate-tool-invocations in our canaries from ~2.3% to <0.1% and clarifies retry guidance for SDKs. See also linked Linear tasks for follow-ups.

title: hybrid flashattn + lazy-kv-eviction + operator-fusion for long-seq workloads
preview:
Motivation: Production workloads with mixed short- and long-context requests show uneven tail latency and high aggregate token cost due to conservative KV cache retention and generic attention kernel selection. This PR introduces a hybrid attention path that (1) dispatches FlashAttention when safe, (2) uses a lightweight fused matmul-add-dropout kernel for mid-sized heads, and (3) falls back to the existing fallback kernel for pathological shapes. In parallel, we implement a lazy KV eviction policy that decouples KV entry lifetime from request completion and enables opportunistic eviction under memory pressure. Together these changes reduce long-sequence p99 latency and improve throughput for streaming workloads while keeping memory bounded.

What changed (high level): implemented a Flash-aware scheduler that picks kernel variant at invocation time based on sequence length, head size, and kv-cache residency; added a fused QKV->matmul+add kernel for medium shapes (mp size 16/32) to reduce kernel launch overhead; integrated a lazy-kv-eviction manager that uses LRU+age heuristics and signals the scheduler to prefer compact kernels when eviction is active; CI/build: updated CUDA kernels, operator registration, and added unit/integration tests; docs: runtime tuning doc and rollout checklist.

Benchmarks (representative): evaluated on 8xA100-80GB and local dev GPU (A5000). Long-seq streaming (seq=16384, batch=1) shows p50 latency -12%, p95 -23%, p99 -31%, tokens/sec +18% vs main with previous FlashAttention dispatch. Medium mixed workload (seq distribution 128-2048) throughput improved +12% and reduced kernel launch counts by ~28%. Memory: average KV memory footprint reduced ~9% due to lazy eviction behavior under synthetic tail traffic.

Testing & CI: unit tests for kernel selection logic, KV eviction invariants, and end-to-end streaming harness. Added synthetic replay tests for real production traces. CI initially failed on quantization edge-case in fused kernel (nvcc error for sm_80 path), fixed by tightening alignments and adding memcpy fallback for odd strides. Full CI now green.

Rollout plan: feature-gated behind runtime.experimental.hybrid_attention. Staged rollout: Canary on Dedicated pool -> 10% Hosted -> 50% -> full. Canary will enable additional tracing hooks for per-request kernel decisions and kv-eviction events. Rollback path: flip feature flag or restart runtime to main kernel selection. The PR does not change public API, no breaking change.

Checklist: - [x] Kernel selection heuristics implemented and unit-tested - [x] Fused kernel validated on sm_70/75/80 - [x] Lazy KV eviction has safety invariants and metrics - [x] End-to-end perf harness and regression alerts - [x] Rollout feature flag and tracing - [x] Docs & tuning guide updated

title: Introduce hierarchical fidelity oracle and non-blocking CI adapter
preview: Motivation: existing CI gates are brittle for prompt sets with heterogeneous lengths, multi-turn contexts and low-frequency anchors which lead to noisy false positives. This PR introduces a hierarchical fidelity oracle that fuses multiple evaluation signals (semantic similarity, role-aware turn consistency, token-level saliency, and anchor stability) across temporal cohorts and prompt granularity, and exposes a non-blocking CI adapter that surfaces soft-fail quality regressions with prioritized remediation suggestions. Core behaviors: 1) compute per-prompt multi-signal fidelity vectors, 2) aggregate per-cohort with recency-aware weighting and uncertainty calibration, 3) emit composite fidelity score and explainability hints (top contributing tokens and failing turns), 4) provide a non-blocking CI decision path which fails only on high-confidence regressions and otherwise annotates PRs with risk tiers and suggested rerun probes. This change contains: implementation of scorer and adapter, integration tests, docs and an example anchor manifest. Checklist: [x] implement hierarchical_fidelity scorer; [x] adapter for CI (non-blocking by default, configurable thresholds); [x] integration tests with synthetic perturbation reruns; [x] docs and example anchor prompt set; [x] Linear links (ENG-4523, ENG-6110) added to PR. Testing notes: local unit and integration tests added; synthetic rerun harness simulates paraphrase/negation/noise and verifies attenuation of false positives vs current thresholded gate; metrics show 22% reduction in spurious CI failures on internal smoke corpus. Rollout plan: gate is opt-in per-repo via a small YAML config in .eval/config.yaml, default mode is "annotate-only"; after two weeks of opt-in telemetry, global adoption can be proposed. text: Motivation: existing CI gates are brittle for prompt sets with heterogeneous lengths, multi-turn contexts and low-frequency anchors which lead to noisy false positives. This PR introduces a hierarchical fidelity oracle that fuses multiple evaluation signals (semantic similarity, role-aware turn consistency, token-level saliency, and anchor stability) across temporal cohorts and prompt granularity, and exposes a non-blocking CI adapter that surfaces soft-fail quality regressions with prioritized remediation suggestions. Core behaviors: 1) compute per-prompt multi-signal fidelity vectors, 2) aggregate per-cohort with recency-aware weighting and uncertainty calibration, 3) emit composite fidelity score and explainability hints (top contributing tokens and failing turns), 4) provide a non-blocking CI decision path which fails only on high-confidence regressions and otherwise annotates PRs with risk tiers and suggested rerun probes. This change contains: implementation of scorer and adapter, integration tests, docs and an example anchor manifest. title: Mini-bisect canary with traffic snapshots and automatic warmstart rollback oracle
preview: Summary: Introduce a lightweight 'mini-bisect' canary mode and traffic snapshotting to speed up diagnosis and automate rollback for warmstart regressions. Motivation: We observed slow, manual bisect workflows when a canary exhibits intermittent regressions related to cache warmstart/kv-cache priming. This PR implements a controller pattern that runs short-window bisect-style probes, records traffic snapshots (input distribution, sequence-length buckets, metadata tags), and evaluates a compact set of metric comparisons to decide an automatic rollback. Design: 1) controller: new MiniBisectController that coordinates N short probe waves (default 3), each wave splits a small, orthogonal slice of traffic to the candidate model. 2) traffic snapshots: proto-compact JSON blobs capturing token-length histograms, request metadata tags, and sampled request hashes; snapshots are stored in the rollout namespace and retained for 48h. 3) warmstart oracle: compares per-wave latency p95, kv-cache hit-rate estimate, and loss-proxy signal (existing quality metrics) against baseline with configurable thresholds and a decay-weighted scoring; if oracle score crosses rollback threshold, controller triggers an immediate revert to previous model and emits forensic snapshot bundle to the audit log. 4) Autoscaling safety: the controller coordinates with scheduler to throttle probe concurrency to avoid autoscaler thrash. 5) Opt-in config: rollout policy field 'mini_bisect: {enabled:true, waves:3, snapshot_ttl:48h}' and feature-flag rollout.mini_bisect.enable. Backwards compatibility: defaults disabled; no surface change to SDKs. Testing: unit tests for snapshot serialization + controller simulator harness; e2e in perf-canary lab using synthetic traffic. Rollout plan: behind feature flag, staged on staging, then limited production on internal customers for 2 weeks. See linked Linear tasks ENG-4812 ENG-4890 for follow-ups.

title: typed errors + ergonomic iterable reconnect API across SDKs
preview:
Motivation: Developers repeatedly hit vague runtime errors when streams failed (auth handshake, transient network) and had to implement ad-hoc parsing logic to decide whether to retry, resume, or surface an error. This PR introduces language-native typed error classes across the three SDKs (Python/TypeScript/Go) and a unified ergonomic iterable reconnect API for stream consumers.

What this change does: 
- Introduces TypedError/StreamError subclasses with machine-friendly error_code, retriable, and suggestion fields across SDKs.
- Adds a new StreamIterable API (python: StreamIterable, ts: createStreamIterable, go: StreamIterator) that yields typed result objects and automatically attempts a single reconnect on retriable failures by default with backoff tuning. Reconnect behavior is configurable by the caller.
- Deprecates low-level sync callback hooks (on_chunk/on_error) in favor of async iterables/promises/iterators to improve ergonomics and parity.
- Adds structured telemetry hooks (opt-in) so apps can key metrics by error_code without brittle string matching.
- Small refactors: move common auth-retry utility into internal/sdk-common, update changelogs and bump SDK package versions.

Backward compatibility and migration: 
- Old callback APIs are still present but marked deprecated; they will emit one deprecation log per process and are scheduled for removal in the next major. Migration notes are added to each SDK CHANGELOG.
- Typed error shapes are additive; existing code catching broad exceptions will continue to work but can opt-in to typed handling for clearer logic.

Testing and rollout: 
- Unit tests added for typed error construction, serialization, and iterable reconnect edge cases. Integration tests simulate transient auth failures with a test double of the runtime.
- Canary rollout to Dedicated region us-west-2 for 72 hours before full merge.

Checklist: 
- [x] Python implementation and tests
- [x] TypeScript implementation and tests
- [x] Go implementation and tests
- [x] Changelogs and version bumps
- [x] Docs snippet in quickstarts repo pointing to new API example

References: Linear ENG-1987, SUP-402 (customer-facing incident), internal design doc: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-1987/stream-reconnect-typed-errors (internal link).

title: Tenant ecoregion slowpath isolation and soft-park emergency stabilization
preview: Summary: Introduces a serving-side scheduler extension that groups tenants into lightweight 'ecoregions' (region + workload fingerprint) and isolates slowpath/large-KV sessions into a soft-park queue when node-level KV pressure threatens SLOs. Motivation: During the Jan incident we observed correlated KV-heavy warm sessions from multiple tenants in the same AZ causing tail latency spikes and subsequent SLO breaches for unrelated low-latency tenants. This change provides a fast mitigation: (1) detect sustained KV pressure per-node and per-ecoregion, (2) move new/remaining slowpath requests for affected ecoregions into a soft-park state (deferred processing + light-weight token), (3) prioritize unmoved requests to preserve 95th/99th latency for interactive tenants, and (4) expose observability + an operator rollback. This PR is scoped for runtime safety and is intended as an emergency stabilization feature that is off by default and gated by config. What changed (high-level): - New scheduler module: serving/scheduler/ecoregion_scheduler.go (ecoregion detection, scoring, admission decisions) - New soft-park runtime: serving/runtime/soft_park.go (park queue, lightweight lease, KV-pressure circuit hooks) - Config knobs: config/defaults.yaml (serving.runtime.ecoregion.enabled=false by default, thresholds, park timeouts) - Metrics & telemetry: metrics/slo_tags.go, console/widgets/ecoregion-telemetry-panel.json - Integration tests: tests/integration/ecoregion_integration_test.go - Operator docs: docs/operating/ecoregion.md Why it's safe: - Feature is opt-in (serving.runtime.ecoregion.enabled=false) and guarded by two-tier checks (node_kv_pressure && ecoregion_spike_score). - Soft-park holds requests with a short, configurable lease (default 5s) that can be expired or promoted; requests are not dropped. - We add a rollout monitor check that will auto-disable the feature if error rates increase > 0.5 percentage points for any tenant in the group during warmup. Rollback plan: immediate toggle via runtime config (console + CLI) to revert to previous behavior. Fast rollback script included in scripts/rollbacks/disable_ecoregion.sh (not changing infra templates). Testing: - Unit tests for scheduler heuristics and soft-park transitions. - Integration tests simulate mixed workloads (low-latency chat + KV-heavy batch) and assert 95p latency preservation for low-latency tenants while soft-parked ecoregions show bounded increased tail. - Canary on dedicated infra: rolled to one canary pool for 12 hours, monitored SLO_ECO_95 and SLO_GLOBAL_99. CI history: initial CI run failed lint (naming) and a flaky integration test; fixed by commit 4 and rerun — final CI passed all checks (build, unit, integration, linters, telemetry smoke). Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed (Linear ENG-4219) - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] Canary rollout completed (1 pool) - [x] Ops doc and console panel - [x] Runtime toggle + rollback script - [x] Metrics and alert hooks (SLO_ECO_95, ECO_SOFTPARK_INFLIGHT) Commits: see commit list below. Merge: squashed and merged to main on 2026-02-27 after approvals. Related: SUP-1123 (customer incident), ENG-4231 (follow-up telemetry work).

title: introduce-slateline-batch-lanes-and-hierarchical-quant-allocator
preview: Motivation: Large fleets see varying request shapes; a single batching policy + flat quant profile leaves money on the table. This PR introduces 'SlateLine' batch lanes and a hierarchical quant allocator that together provide per-tenant/per-workload batch shaping and a priority-ranked quant fallback tree to reduce unit cost while respecting SLOs. Design summary: SlateLine divides the serving runtime batcher into lightweight lanes (fast-latency lane, balanced lane, throughput lane) with a tiny per-lane selector that uses recent shape histograms and token budget hints to pick the lane for each request. The hierarchical quant allocator maps model variants into a tree of quantization profiles: top nodes are high-fidelity (FP16/quant8-mixed) and leaves are aggressive int4/int2 variants. At runtime the allocator prefers higher-fidelity nodes but can step down along the tree when budget or capacity policies trigger. Key behaviors implemented: 1) lane selector service integrated into serving runtime (stable, lock-free sampling path), 2) lane-specific batching windows and kv-cache sharing limits, 3) quant allocator with soft budget quotas and graceful degradation path, 4) telemetry hooks for unit-econ attribution (tokens, lane, quant-level), 5) rollout flags and tenant override controls. Migration and safety: rollout gated by feature flag "slateline_enabled" per-cluster; default off. A conservative rollout profile named "slateline-conservative" is included which only enables lanes and allocator simulation (no quant fallbacks) to collect metrics for 24-72h. A second progressive profile enables live allocator step-downs with strict latency SLO enforcement and automatic revert if error or p95 latency regressions exceed thresholds. Testing: unit tests for allocator tree traversal, integration tests that run synthetic workloads exercising the three lanes, e2e perf test harness added to perf-canary to simulate tenant traffic and assert p50/p95 tail behavior and cost delta. CI: flaky integration test fixed by a deterministic seeding of the request sampler. Observability: new metrics: redwood.slateline.lane_chosen, redwood.slateline.quant_level, redwood.slateline.alloc_decisions, and taggable labels for unit_econ (tenant_id, route). Documentation: console docs and API playground snippets added under redwood-docs to explain flags and tenant override API. Checklist: - [x] design doc reviewed (ENG-4312) - [x] unit+integration tests added - [x] perf harness entry for canary - [x] feature flags and rollout profiles - [x] telemetry and cost attribution hooks - [x] docs and CLI flag wiring Migration notes: Operators can test in simulation mode by enabling slateline_enabled=false and slateline_simulate=true; this will emit allocator decisions without altering quant levels. Live fallback thresholds are configurable via the console and via the cluster config file under runtime/batcher/slateline.yaml. Rollout plan: 1) enable simulation in canary region for 48h, 2) conservative flag in 2 small tenants for 72h, 3) progressive enable on low-risk tenants for 1 week, 4) full enable with automatic revert. Impact: internal perf-canary runs show roughly 7-12% token-cost reduction on mixed workloads with <2% p95 latency delta using conservative policies; aggressive policy hits 18-25% savings at cost of slightly higher p50/95 in synthetic throughput bursts. Related work: complements recent tenant-prefill and quant-profile recommendations; differs by pushing decision logic into the runtime and providing lane-aware batching rather than global batch windows. CI and post-merge tasks: tag release, update canary scenarios, watch the unit_econ dashboard and alert on cost regressions.

title: Introduce jurisdictional operator claims, KMS proxy with region affinity, and audit canonicalizer
preview: Motivation: operator-initiated requests that used fallback KMS routes could violate tenant residency expectations in multi-geozone deployments. This PR introduces a lightweight KMS proxy with region-affinity routing and a jurisdictional operator-claim propagation middleware that ties RBAC v2 decisions to geozone claims. It also adds an audit header canonicalizer to ensure consistent downstream proofs for compliance ingestion. Changes include a claim backfill migration for existing operator sessions and telemetry for residency decision sampling. Checklist: - [x] design doc and Linear ticket ENG-4521 linked, - [x] RBAC v2 policy and policy tests, - [x] KMS proxy and unit tests, - [x] migration/backfill job and smoke tests, - [x] staged rollout flag + installer hook, - [ ] manual staging canary and runbook signoff. Design references: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4521 and internal design note https://github.com/redwood/internal/designs/kms-region-affinity.md. Rollout plan: feature gate on by default in staging, opt-in via installer flag for dedicated/private customers while telemetry is monitored for 72h before full enablement.

title: unify-toolchain-bootstraps-and-introduce-lightweight-presubmit
preview: Motivation: Our repo currently has three slightly different developer bootstraps (lint, unit-test, integration-smoke) implemented as independent scripts and Dockerfiles which diverged over time. This PR consolidates those bootstraps into a single lightweight, hermetic toolchain image and a small Go-based presubmit runner which selects the minimal runtime surface at CI time. Goals: reduce duplication, speed cold CI starts, make dependency updates atomic, and simplify contributor onboarding. What this change does: 1) Introduces tools/toolchain/manifest.yaml describing canonical toolchain components and pins (node, go, python, buildx). 2) Adds images/toolchain/Dockerfile which builds a single slim toolchain image used by lint and test runners. 3) Replaces three bash bootstraps with cmd/presubmit-runner (Go) which performs staged execution: install-verify -> lint -> unit-tests -> integration-smoke with fast-skip heuristics. 4) Updates GitHub Actions workflows: .github/workflows/presubmit.yaml now has a lightweight presubmit lane that uses archived container layers and path-aware job gating. 5) Adds compatibility shim scripts/devcontainer-compat.sh for older local dev setups. 6) Consolidates dependency pinning into tools/toolchain/lockfiles/ with a single update script and a health-check action that flags transitive drift. 7) Small docs update in docs/developer/ci.md and a migration note for contributors. Rollout: staged canary on small PRs followed by full switch on main after 48h of green presubmit passing. Backout plan: re-enable previous bootstraps by reverting the action and image tag; migration notes included under docs/developer/ci.md. Checklist: [x] toolchain image build and publish step added to ci-images job [x] presubmit Go runner unit tests [x] integration-smoke stability tests in nightly [x] devcontainer compatibility shim added [x] docs updated Related work: ENG-2145 (presubmit redesign), ENG-2198 (dep-pinning unification). Links: design doc https://linear.app/redwood/board/ENG-2145/design/bootstrapping-toolchain Notes: this is intentionally conservative about changing job semantics; the presubmit runner defaults to existing behavior unless FASTPATH env is set. Expected outcomes: median cold job startup improved ~18% observed in the canary, cache hit rate improved due to single image reuse, and fewer transient failures from mismatched tool versions.

title: Introduce epochal log tiling and cost-gradient alert smoother
preview:
Motivation: Recent incidents showed noisy long-tail log retention and spiky cost alerts that produced pager fatigue. This PR implements two complementary subsystems to reduce noise while preserving fidelity for root-cause: 1) epochal log tiling: a retention/compaction scheme that buckets invocation logs into time-anchored 'epochs' and keeps variable-fidelity tiles (high-fidelity for recent epochs, compacted digests for older epochs). 2) cost-gradient alert smoother: an adaptive alert thresholding algorithm that considers the short-term gradient of per-tenant token cost to suppress transient spikes and surface persistent regressions.

What this PR changes (summary): - New epochal tiler microservice (observability/log-tiler) with configurable epoch length and tile retention policy. - Retention controller that coordinates between object store (S3/GCS) and hot cache to keep recent tiles at full resolution. - Token-correlated lightweight phase metrics (metrics/token_phase_sampler) to enable low-cardinality attribution of where cost changes originate (prompt/response/kv-cache). - Cost-gradient alerting library (alerts/cost_gradient) with hysteresis and adaptive dampening; integrates into existing SLO pipeline. - New Grafana dashboard panels and an operator guide (docs/observability/epochal-tiling.md). - Unit and integration tests + upgrade notes.

title: Fold per-layer scale into inline requantizer; attention KV-cache compatibility
preview: Motivation: INT8 and FP8 paths pay a per-token cost for explicit scale/dequant round-trips in the attention kernel. This PR folds stable per-layer/chunk scaling factors into an inline requantizer inside the GEMM/attention kernels and adds compatibility glue for our attention KV-cache (so cached keys/values don't trigger extra conversions on read). Goals: reduce per-token arithmetic, improve single-stream latency, and keep cached tensors bit-compatible across quant/float fallbacks. Implementation summary: 1) introduce a lightweight FoldedScale descriptor that is emitted at model-load time and stored alongside quant metadata; 2) implement inline_requantizer.{cc,hh} which applies scale folding + rounding once on kernel entry and keeps activations in the requantized layout for the kernel duration; 3) modify attention KV load/store paths to check for FoldedScale and avoid redundant dequant->requant conversions when a cached shard already carries the folded scale tag; 4) add a guarded FP8 stochastic-round option (config flag) to preserve edge-case numerics for very small-scale layers; 5) update the continuous batcher to prefer grouping requests by compatible folded-scale signatures to maximize kernel reuse. Testing & benchmarks: unit tests added (tests/quant/scale_fold_test.cc) to validate numerical equivalence within target tolerances; end-to-end perf bench (bench/quant/bench_int8_fold.py) shows 8-14% single-stream latency reduction on A100-80GB with INT8 kernels and 6-9% throughput improvement in mixed-batch workloads when continuous batching groups by folded-scale. Backwards compatibility: when FoldedScale metadata is absent, kernels fall back to the previous explicit dequant/requant code path. Migration: model loader emits folded-scale for models that pass a short calibration pass; opt-out via REDWOOD_QUANT_NO_FOLD env var. Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked in Linear ENG-1289 - [x] Unit tests - [x] Perf benchmarks attached - [x] KV-cache compatibility glue - [x] Feature gated behind config flags and env opt-out - [x] Docs: docs/perf/quant-ops.md updated Notes: this is intentionally conservative — we avoid changing on-disk weight formats and only tag cache entries with a small folded-scale id. The approach aims to reduce runtime arithmetic without altering model fidelity. Related: ENG-1344 (continuous-batching grouping by quant-signature).

title: phase-shifted-latency-sweep-and-coldstart-prober
preview:
Motivation: We need a lightweight, reproducible runner that exercises both steady-state and phase-shifted burst patterns while simultaneously measuring cold-start latency across different model variants and runtime kernel configs. This PR introduces a phase-shifted latency sweep runner and a paired cold-start prober that can be composed to produce joint distributions (latency vs cold-start tail) used by the regression detector pipeline.
Design:
- phase_sweep_runner: Python runner that generates a sequence of workload phases with configurable duration, RPS, sequence length distribution and an adjustable phase-offset parameter to produce controlled interleaving across client shards.
- coldstart_prober: small Go probe that issues warmup-less single-shot requests on a configurable model selection (quantized/FP16/FP32) to capture cold-start percentiles under concurrent load.
- reporter bridge: new CSV+JSON reporter that tags samples with phase-id, shard-id, kernel-profile and coldstart-epoch for downstream aggregation.
- CI harness changes: bench job integration and a perf-regression threshold test that runs an abbreviated sweep in CI with synthetic small-models.
How to run: bench/README contains examples; typical run: python bench/runners/phase_sweep_runner.py --profile profiles/low_mem.yaml --phases 6 --phase-duration 30s --phase-offset 7s --out reports/run-<ts>.csv
The coldstart probe is compiled with go build and invoked alongside the runner to annotate early-epoch samples.
Implementation notes:
- Uses existing bench client abstraction for request generation and adds a deterministic pseudo-random seed to make runs reproducible.
- Phase IDs are encoded into the request metadata header X-Bench-Phase so runtime traces can correlate tokens to phase.
- Reporter writes a compact JSON lines stream and a summary CSV compatible with the regression detector.
- Adds a small integration test that validates phase interleaving logic and a smoke perf check for the probe binary.
Commits:
- "initial phase_sweep_runner implementation, core API and simple CLI"
- "add coldstart_prober (go) and CI smoke job"
- "reporter: csv + jsonlines bridge and header tagging"
- "docs: bench user guide and example profiles"
- "fix: deterministic seeding + durable temp file cleanup"
Files changed (high level):
- bench/runners/phase_sweep_runner.py (new): core runner, phase scheduler, sampling hooks
- bench/probers/coldstart_prober/main.go (new): standalone probe binary
- bench/reporters/csv_json_bridge.go (new): reporter bridge for CSV/JSONL outputs
- bench/configs/profiles/phase_sweep_low_mem.yaml (new): example profile
- integration/test_phase_sweep.py (new): lightweight unit/integration checks
- docs/bench/phase-sweep.md (new): usage and interpretation
- ci/bench.yml (modified): adds perf sweep smoke job
Reviewers requested small API changes (explicit --seed flag, clearer exit codes for the probe) and asked for a more conservative default for phase-offset to avoid self-synchronization artifacts on noisy CI hosts. I iterated on the runner CLI, added --seed and made the default phase-offset randomized within a small jitter when running in CI mode.
Tests: unit tests for scheduler logic and an integration runner simulation were added.
CI summary:
- build: pass
- unit-tests: pass
- integration-tests: pass
- bench-smoke: pass (flaky on first run; retried once)
- perf-regression-check: pass (abbreviated)
Merge: squashed and merged to main.
Notes on impact and usage:
- This enables the benchmark lab to capture correlated latency tailing behavior during workload phase transitions and quantify cold-start exposure for short-lived containers.
- New CSV schema is backwards-compatible with the regression detector after a small mapping layer; a follow-up will add direct ingestion.
Checklist:
- [x] Runner implementation
- [x] Coldstart probe
- [x] Reporter bridge and CSV schema
- [x] CI integration
- [x] Docs and examples
- [x] Tests and smoke checks
Related: ENG-4821 tracks adding cold-start monitoring to the nightly suites; ENG-4890 is a follow-up to integrate these runs into the SLA dashboard.

title: Add Cost Model API Grafana dashboards (latency/errors, schema version dist, drift)
preview: Summary: Adds a Grafana folder + dashboards for the Cost Model API so Console/Optimize rollouts have a single place to monitor health, adoption, and correctness drift. Includes panels for request volume, p50/p95/p99 latency, error-rate by code, schema version distribution, request-bounds rejections, cache hit/miss (client-side + service memoization), and a first pass of drift metrics (predicted vs observed) sourced from the scheduled drift job. Motivation: During early Console integration testing we saw two recurring issues: (1) latency spikes due to cardinality blowups on hot paths and (2) drift alerts firing due to telemetry lag vs genuine mismatch. This PR standardizes the dashboards and bakes in guardrail signals (telemetry freshness, missing inputs) so oncall can quickly triage. Related: redwood/pr-28457 (service endpoints), redwood/pr-28490 (drift monitoring job), redwood/pr-28495 (what-if guardrails), SUP-31905 (latency spike report). What’s included (dashboards): 1) "Cost Model API / Overview" (high-level SLO views): RPS, concurrency, saturation, p50/p95/p99 latency, error budget burn (5m/1h/6h), top tenants (bounded), and a "schema negotiation" panel showing unknown/unsupported versions returned to clients. 2) "Cost Model API / Errors & Bounds" (debug): error rate split by status, error_code, and stage; panels for bound rejections (e.g., breakdown_group_by too high-cardinality), request payload size buckets, and timeouts by caller (console-backend vs optimize-service). 3) "Cost Model API / Schema Version Adoption": distribution of requested schema_version vs served schema_version (incl. fallback), percent of clients sending optional fields for cache/batching/quantization; includes a heatmap of version by environment. 4) "Cost Model / Drift": predicted_cost_usd vs observed_cost_usd (daily + hourly), drift pct, confidence bands where available, and triage panels: telemetry lag (ingestion watermark age), missing-runtime-input rate, and "prefill/decode split present" ratio. Alerts: Adds two alert rules (behind the existing oncall routing policy): (a) p95 latency > 300ms for 10m (scoped to prod + excluding canary envs), (b) drift_pct > 5% for 6h AND telemetry_lag < 15m (to reduce false positives during pipeline delays). Notes: Drift is expected to be noisy early; the dashboard intentionally surfaces "telemetry freshness" next to drift so oncall can distinguish pipeline lag from real regression. Implementation details: Dashboards live under grafana/dashboards/cost-model-api/*.jsonnet and are rendered to JSON in CI. Metrics consumed assume the cost-model service exports: cost_model_api_request_duration_seconds (histogram), cost_model_api_requests_total (by status, error_code, caller, env), cost_model_api_schema_version_served_total, and drift job exports: cost_model_drift_ratio, cost_model_drift_pct, cost_model_observed_cost_usd, cost_model_predicted_cost_usd, telemetry_watermark_age_seconds. Rollout: Once merged, SRE will deploy the updated observability pack on the next release train; Console rollout doc will link to these dashboards. Screenshots: Added to the internal docs PR separately (redwood-docs/pr-1187) to avoid bloating this repo.

title: Instrument kernel-level latency fingerprints and introduce adaptive kernel-fingerprint alerting
preview:
Motivation: We see occasional long-tail invocation latency where root cause is GPU kernel scheduling/serialization rather than model token path. Existing observability surfaces capture per-invocation token timing but lack a compact way to correlate GPU kernel-level skew across tenants and hardware. This PR instruments low-level kernel timing as compact 'fingerprints', surfaces a kernel heatmap dashboard, and introduces an adaptive alert that suppresses noise during autoscale events.

What this PR does (high-level):
- Adds runtime span attributes: kernel_name, kernel_ms, gpu_sm_id, kernel_queue_depth. Spans are emitted by the serving runtime when kernels are dispatched.
- Emits a new metric series: telemetry.kernel_fingerprint with labels {model_family, hw_class, kernel_name, tenant_id? (opt)}, using reservoir sampling to limit cardinality.
- Adds a Kernel Heatmap dashboard (dashboards/kernel-heatmap.json) that shows per-kernel p50/p95/p99 across HW classes and time-of-day.
- Introduces an alert rule (alerts/kernel-fingerprint-drift.yaml) that computes drift against a rolling baseline and only fires when drift correlates with stable capacity (i.e., not during autoscale events) to reduce false positives.
- Adds a short-lived debug mode (env var REDWOOD_KERNEL_FINGERPRINT_DEBUG) that collects per-chunk latency samples into a ring buffer for on-demand upload during incidents.
- Adds tests for metric tagging and an integration test that mocks kernel span emission and confirms the heatmap ingestion pipeline.

Checklist:
- [x] Instrument runtime spans
- [x] Metric export + reservoir sampling
- [x] Dashboard JSON
- [x] Alert rule and suppression logic
- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Docs: added a short guide in docs/observability/kernel-fingerprints.md

Commits (summary):
- Add kernel span attrs and exporter hooks (sof-2026/kernel-span-export)
- Implement reservoir sampler for kernel_fingerprint series (telemetry/core)
- Add dashboard JSON and panels for kernel heatmap and per-hw kernel table
- Add alert rule with autoscale-aware suppression and test harness
- Add docs and e2e test to simulate skew and verify suppression

Files changed (high-level):
- runtime/dispatch/kernel_instrumentation.go (new)
- telemetry/metrics/kernel_sampler.go (new)
- telemetry/pipelines/kernel_ingest_pipeline.go (modified)
- observability/dashboards/kernel-heatmap.json (new)
- observability/alerts/kernel-fingerprint-drift.yaml (new)

title: consolidate-premerge-linters-and-ci-shepherd-for-dep-trimming
preview: Motivation: our presubmit plane has accumulated 6 separate linter/test entrypoints that slow fast feedback and confuse contributors. This PR centralizes linting into a single lightweight 'ci-shepherd' composite action, migrates legacy shell-run linters to a typed Node wrapper, and introduces a dependent-presubmit trim step that excludes irrelevant packages from heavyweight integration tests. Changeset summary: (1) new actions/ci-shepherd composite with stage-aware lanes (fast-lint, slow-lint, unit-smoke), (2) migrate eslint/remark invocations into the node-based runner to allow deterministic caching and telemetry, (3) add .ci/dep-trim/indexer that computes minimal test matrix from changed files (opt-in via PR label), (4) update docs/CONTRIBUTING.md and devcontainer instructions, (5) pin a handful of devdeps and update lockfiles to reduce transient failures. Why this matters: median presubmit time for small repos dropped from ~6m to ~90s for fast-lint; heavyweight integrational lanes run only when dep-trim signals touching infra or runtime code. Rollout plan: staged rollout behind feature flag in workflows, opt-in on a subset of teams, run in shadow for 48h, then roll global. Related: ENG-8821 (presubmit feedback), ENG-8840 (dep-trim spec).

title: v1/chat: output coordinator + role-pruner for compact structured tool responses
preview:
Motivation: customers relying on OpenAI /v1/chat.completions compatibility have increasingly complex tool/function outputs and varied role labels from legacy clients. The streaming path was producing verbose, repeated JSON fragments for tool responses and leaking low-confidence token-level logprobs during long streams. This PR introduces an 'output coordinator' layer in the v1 chat adapter and a conservative 'role-pruner' to normalize incoming roles and emit a compact structured mode for tool/function outputs.

Summary of changes: added an adapter-level coordinator that: 1) recognizes and collapses repeated tool JSON fragments into a single compact structure when clients request compact output (new query param compact_output=true), 2) supplies a configurable stop_hint_priority so that explicit stop sequences sent by the API call are respected ahead of model-internal heuristics when desired, and 3) filters streaming logprob deltas below a configurable threshold to reduce bandwidth and surface only meaningful token confidence changes. The role-pruner maps unknown or noisy role labels (e.g. "assistant_bot", "system_user") into canonical roles (system, user, assistant) while preserving an optional role_metadata bag in streaming envelopes. The default mapping is conservative to avoid breaking customers who depend on custom roles.

API surface: new v1/chat query params (all optional, backward compatible): compact_output (boolean) - when true, server collapses repeated tool JSON fragments and returns a single 'tool_result' object per tool invocation; compact_mode (string) - "compact"|"verbose" controls how much ancillary metadata is included; stop_hint_weight (float 0-1) - higher values prefer explicit stop param over model-internal stops; logprob_filter_threshold (float 0-1) - tokens with probability below threshold will have logprobs suppressed in streaming deltas to reduce bandwidth. The existing 'function_call' behavior is unchanged unless compact_output is enabled.

Runtime changes: introduced runtime/stream/coordinator.py to manage stream fragment coalescing with backpressure awareness, integrated compacting logic into the v1 adapter to avoid double-encoding, and added a small heuristic to avoid coalescing across user-turn boundaries. Implemented a lightweight role-pruner in server/adapter/openai_compat/v1_chat_adapter.py that consults a normalization table and emits a role_hints field on the streaming envelope to surface pruned-original-role for observability.

Compatibility and safety: defaults preserve current behavior (compact_output=false, stop_hint_weight=0.5, logprob_filter_threshold=0.01). Role-pruner requires an opt-in flag (role_prune=true) for strict normalization; otherwise we only emit role_hints. The change intentionally avoids rewriting function signatures or existing response envelopes to prevent breaking existing integrations.

Testing and benchmarks: added integration tests under tests/integration/test_toolflow_streaming.py and unit tests for coordinator heuristics. Benchmarks run on perf-canary showed ~9-12% reduction in outbound streaming tokens for workloads with verbose tool JSONs (internal benchmark workload 'toolsuite-large-2025'), and ~3-6% average reduction in end-to-end latency due to fewer serialization events.

Docs and migration: updated docs/openai_compat.md with example calls, recommended migration path, and a sample shim for SDKs to opt-in to compact_output gradually. Release notes include guidance on when to enable compact_output and how to tune logprob_filter_threshold for telemetry vs fidelity.

Checklist: - [x] Runtime coordinator implementation + unit tests - [x] Role-pruner with opt-in flag - [x] SDK sample snippet and doc update - [x] Perf benchmark and canary run - [x] Security review for envelope changes

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the compact representation as an opt-in addition. We observed a handful of legacy clients that relied on repeated fragments for incremental parsing; for those customers we recommend gradual migration with compact_output=false while testing SDK helpers that reconstruct streamed fragments into a compact object on the client side.

title: generate delta upgrade index and SBOM stamping for private-installer CI
preview: This PR adds a CI-first pipeline to produce deterministic delta upgrade packages and stamp SBOM/provenance metadata into the private-installer release artifacts. Motivation: customers running Private (VPC/on-prem) need compact upgrade bundles and a verifiable upgrade graph so operators can perform safe roll-forwards and rollbacks without re-pulling full images. The output of the pipeline is a signed delta-index (delta-index.json) that expresses available upgrade paths between installer versions and references per-artifact SBOMs, Helm chart lockfiles, and Terraform module plan snapshots. Changes include: 1) new pipeline job (private-installer:delta-pack) that builds compact tarballs containing only changed manifests and image digests; 2) delta-indexer tool (tools/release/delta-indexer.go) which computes graph edges and emits SBOM references; 3) SBOM stamping step using syft and a lightweight metadata envelope; 4) cosign/cosign-wrapper integration to attach OCI signatures and timestamped provenance to each delta bundle; 5) Helm chart lockfile generator (charts/lockfiles/) and Terraform module plan snapshotter (terraform/modules/plan-snapshots/); 6) makefile and CI matrix additions to validate deltas with a staging smoke deploy and terraform plan dry-run. Checklist: - [x] implement delta-index format and generator - [x] wire up CI job and pipeline config - [x] add SBOM generation and envelope signing - [x] add unit tests for indexer and SBOM parsing - [x] add e2e smoke test in staging workflow - [x] document format and CLI usage in docs/release/delta-index.md Backwards compatibility: existing full-installer artifacts and release flow are unchanged; the delta-index is additive and consumed by the private-installer CLI/upgrade controller. Note on security: SBOMs are generated with syft v0.83.0 and emitted alongside artifacts; cosign keys are stored in the CI KMS and rotated per release cadence. See linked Linear tickets for rollout plan and gating criteria.

title: Granular SLO fallback credit allocator and soft cutover for regional failovers
preview: Context: Recent incidents showed that coarse global failover decisions cause blunt throttles where small tenants receive disproportionate evictions, and noisy tenants exhaust regional failover budgets leading to cascading circuit trips. This PR introduces a per-tenant 'fallback credit' allocator and a soft cutover pathway for failover windows so regional handoffs can be gradual and observable. Motivation: provide finer-grained SLO enforcement during overloads, reduce noisy-tenant blast radius, and allow gradual regional traffic rebalancing without immediate hard deny or full circuit flips. Summary of changes: 1) implement fallback_credit_store (in-memory sharded) that tracks per-tenant credits consumed during soft-fallback windows; credits replenish on a tunable epoch and are weighted by tenant SLO tier and recent success rate; 2) add a credit-aware admission filter in the regional routing mesh that reduces accepted burst fraction rather than failing immediately when SLO budget is low; 3) implement soft_cutover policy that transitions traffic from primary->fallback targets in 10% increments over a configurable window (default 60s) with telemetry checkpoints; 4) add exportable metrics and traces: slack_credit_ratio, soft_cutover_progress, credit_eviction_count, tenant_requeue_count; 5) configuration knobs exposed via runtime config and Console: per-tenant base_credit, weight_multiplier_by_tier, soft_cutover_step_ms, soft_cutover_step_pct; 6) include end-to-end tests and a load-test harness scenario. Rollout plan: staged rollout to Canary -> Regional canaries (us-west-2) -> 25% of production traffic behind a feature flag per region -> full rollout after 48h rollback window. Metrics to monitor: P50/P95/P99 latency, regional SLO breach counts, tenant-level evictions, credit depletion rate, soft_cutover success rate. Backout: feature flag to disable credit allocation and immediate revert to pre-PR routing on critical regressions. Related: ENG-4821 (SLO enforcement refinement), ENG-4902 (regional failover stability).
