title: fp8-autotune-dequant-rewrite-and-kernel-router
preview:
Motivation: We observed cache-sensitive and sequence-length-dependent regressions when serving FP8/INT8 model variants on the high-throughput path. Two root causes surfaced in profiling: (1) redundant dequantization stubs inserted by the compiler leading to extra memory traffic across the KV cache boundary, and (2) suboptimal kernel selection for mixed FP8/INT8 attention matmuls at mid-sequence lengths, which increases both latency tail and per-token GPU utilization. This PR introduces an autotuned FP8 dequant rewrite pass (dequant-rewriter) plus a scheduler-aware kernel router that picks kernels based on runtime signals (sequence position, KV cache hit pattern, and batching profile).

Summary of changes:
- Implement dequant-rewriter: a graph-level pass that coalesces adjacent dequant ops, hoists dequantization out of hot KV cache paths when safe, and emits a fused "dequant+transpose" kernel where applicable. The pass supports conservative aliasing checks and a feature-flagged faster mode that assumes stable KV shapes in Dedicated deployments.
- FP8 autotuner: offline calibration tool and lightweight runtime probe that chooses between FP8-fused kernels, hybrid FP8/INT8 kernels, or fallback FP16 kernels per model and per-device. Tuner's decisions are persisted in model-serving metadata and can be overridden by rollout flags.
- Kernel router: scheduler integration that consumes continuous-batching statistics (batch size distribution, arrival jitter) and KV cache telemetry to choose kernel variants at request-time. Router minimizes kernel switches within a batch and prefers kernels that maintain low memory traffic across KV boundaries.
- Scheduler hints: expose a small set of hints (seq_pos_bucket, kv_hot_ratio, batch_shape_key) from the serving runtime to the kernel selector. These are lightweight scalar tags attached to batch descriptors.
- Tests and regressions: microbenchmarks for gbench and internal canary; unit tests for the dequant-rewriter; end-to-end perf test in the perf-canary workflow.
- Documentation: notes in docs/serving/quantization.md and a new troubleshooting section describing common FP8 failure modes and mitigation.

Design notes and rationale:
- We avoided aggressive whole-graph FP8 lowering because (a) some models use dynamic dispatch patterns incompatible with a single lowering, and (b) dequantization hoisting across KV cache write-back needs conservative checks to avoid stale keys. The rewriter therefore applies targeted fusion and hoisting, with a safe fallback to existing dequant ops when aliasing cannot be proven.
- Autotuner workflow: an offline pass analyzes representative prompts to build a candidate set. At runtime, a short probe (10s of warmup tokens) validates latency/throughput and writes a per-model best-variant tag. For private and dedicated tenants, the faster autotune mode is enabled by default; hosted tenants require opt-in.

Benchmarks (NVIDIA A100s, sequence mix representative of chat workloads):
- Baseline: mixed FP16 path (pre-change)
  - P50 latency: 86ms
  - P95 latency: 220ms
  - Tokens/sec: 35k
- Post-change (autotuned FP8-fused kernels + dequant-rewriter):
  - P50 latency: 72ms (16% reduction)
  - P95 latency: 165ms (25% reduction)
  - Tokens/sec: 45k (28% increase)
- For small-batch/high-jitter scenarios the kernel router avoided pathological kernel switches and reduced tail variance by ~30%.

Cost impact: in Dedicated benchmarks the improvements correspond to ~22% lower token-GPU-hours for the measured workload mix.

Rollout plan and safety:
- Feature-flagged under runtime.feature.fp8_autotune; default off for hosted mainline for 48h while we collect telemetry.
- Canary deployment to perf-canary and one Dedicated customer (internal) for 72h.
- Automatic rollback policy: if P99 latency increases by >10% or error rate increases by >0.5% over baseline within a 10m window, the rollout controller reverts to the previous kernel tag.

CI and validation:
- Unit and integration tests added under runtime/quant and serving/scheduler.
- Perf canary runs showed initial flakiness due to an aliasing edge-case; fixed by strengthening the alias analysis in the rewriter.
- CI: initial run failed one flaky test (serve_kv_integration_test) and was rerun: final CI passed.

Migration and compatibility notes:
- Models that embed custom kernels may require a one-line model metadata opt-in: "quant.auto_tune: true". We added a compatibility probe that logs recommended kernels without changing runtime behavior until opt-in.
- No API surface change to the Redwood public API.

Observability:
- New metrics: quant_kernel.variant_chosen, quant_autotune.probe_latency, quant_dequant.fused_ops.
- Traces: added tags for seq_pos_bucket and kernel_variant to GPU traces to make regressions easier to root-cause.

Related work: closes ENG-4231 (autotune baseline), progresses ENG-4278 (KV-aware scheduling).

Commits summary: see commits field below.

Files changed (high level): see changed_files field below.

Follow-ups:
- Add FP8 support to the GPU profiler export (ENG-4305).
- Expand autotune dataset to include longer-form decoding patterns.
- Evaluate similar rewriter for INT8-only models where dequantization churn is observed.

title: Dependency-aware rollout orchestrator with safety chains and cohort quarantine
preview:
Motivation: We frequently run multi-model experiments (A/B tests, shadow+canary hybrids) where some models are dependent on upstream feature transformations or other model variants. Current rollouts treat each variant independently which can lead to invalid traffic splits and unsafe rollbacks when dependent cohorts are promoted or quarantined. This PR introduces a dependency-aware rollout orchestrator that models 'safety chains' between rollout nodes so that promotion/rollback decisions are propagated deterministically across related cohorts.

Summary of changes: Adds a new orchestration component (dependency orchestrator) to the servegraph pipeline, implements safety-chain semantics, and extends the rollout policy language with two new directives: "requires" (declare dependencies) and "quarantine_on_failure" (automatic cohort isolation). The orchestrator supports: 1) transactional commit semantics for multi-cohort promotions, 2) automatic quarantine propagation (if a dependent cohort fails SLOs, its dependents are quarantined), 3) staged A/B tie-breaking rules when cohorts share traffic windows, and 4) integration points for Redwood Console to visualize dependency graphs and quarantine status.

Compatibility/migration: Existing rollout specs remain valid; new behaviors are opt-in via the 'dependency_mode: enabled' flag on rollout configs. For teams that want automatic dependency inference, we provide a best-effort heuristic that can be toggled off. No breaking API changes; CLI/SDK support for the new directives added in separate SDK PRs.

Testing: Unit coverage for orchestration logic, integration harness that runs multi-cohort simulations (synthetic latency/error distributions), and a canary e2e with a shadowed dependent model running in the perf-canary lab. A flaky quota test was retried in CI; final green run attached.

Operational notes: When dependency_mode is enabled, redeploys create a transient 'planning' phase for up to 30s where traffic is frozen while the orchestrator evaluates commit plans. This avoids partial promotions. We also emit new metrics: rollout.dependency.plan_duration, rollout.dependency.quarantine_count, and rollout.dependency.propagation_errors. Documented in the rollout runbook and console changelog.

Checklist:
- [x] design doc (linked in ENG-1782)
- [x] unit + integration tests
- [x] console visualization hooks
- [x] migration path and docs

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the orchestration core separate from the scheduling/batching runtime to preserve low-latency paths for token serving. Follow-up PRs will add autoscale-aware ordering for promotions to minimize capacity churn.

title: openai-v1-chat-params-clarity-and-toolcall-debounce-for-stream-stability
preview:
Motivation: We continue to close the gap between Redwood's /v1/chat compatibility surface and the OpenAI chat/completions expectations. Customers reported two classes of issues: (1) noisy rapid fncall footprints during network retries and websocket reconnects that caused multiple tool invocations, and (2) ambiguous behavior when multiple stop signals, role aliases, or logprob fallbacks co-occurred in streaming output. This PR implements two coordinated changes: (A) a parameter clarity layer that normalizes and documents v1 chat params (role aliases, stop token semantics, logprob backoff) so downstream stages get a deterministic shape; (B) a lightweight toolcall debounce and stream heartbeat handshake that prevents duplicated function/tool invocations on transient reconnects and provides a clearer stream parity signal to clients. 

What changed (high level):
- Added a v1 param canonicalizer that: maps role aliases (e.g. assistant/assistant_bot -> assistant), coercively flattens stop lists into prioritized sequences, and exposes an explicit logprob fallback strategy field so the runtime can decide deterministic fallback ordering.
- Introduced a ToolCallDebouncer component (in the streaming runtime) that groups function call events within a short, configurable window and emits a single canonical tool invocation when the payloads are equivalent or supersets. Debounce window defaults to 150ms; policy is configurable per-route.
- Implemented a StreamHeartbeat handshake token inserted into the framed streaming envelope to help clients reason about stream continuity and replay semantics. This is backward-compatible: older clients will ignore the heartbeat key.

title: Add bridged on-call context and automated escalation templates
preview: Motivation: on-call responders frequently need full incident context when incidents are bridged across multiple chat channels and paging systems; manual copy/paste is error-prone and slows TTR. This PR introduces a compact, serializable "bridged context" snapshot that travels with the incident across channels and an opinionated library of parameterized escalation templates that the bot can surface or send automatically. Changes include: 1) context capture: snapshot of current runbook pointer, top-3 alerts, recent error rates, and active mitigations; 2) context propagation: add signed metadata token to bridged messages so downstream responders receive identical context; 3) template library: YAML-driven templates with conditional rendering, throttling and dedupe keys; 4) escalation rules: configurable selectors that map alert signals + SLO breach state to template choices and escalation targets; 5) telemetry and auditing: new metrics and audit entries for every template render/send; 6) feature gating: rollout via feature flag per-org with safe defaults. Backwards compatibility: if a downstream bot doesn't understand the token, it ignores it and nothing breaks; templates are opt-in per-oncall rotation. Testing: unit and integration harness simulating bridged Slack<->PagerDuty flows, contract tests for token validity, and an e2e smoke in staging. Rollout: enable for internal SRE rotation (10% of incidents) for 48h, monitor metrics, then expand to all teams. See ENG-4021 for design notes and SUP-912 for support runbook updates. Checklist: [x] design doc linked [x] unit tests [x] integration tests [x] telemetry added [x] security review requested [x] staged rollout planned.

title: decompose-test-profiles-and-add-dependency-triage-bot
preview:
Title: Decompose test profiles and add dependency triage bot

Description: This PR refactors CI test sharding into small, profile-aware shards and introduces an automated dependency triage bot that opens recommended bump PRs and labels candidate repos for manual review. Motivation: long CI queues and noisy full-suite runs made iteration expensive. By splitting test shards by runtime characteristics (fast/unit, medium/integration, slow/e2e) and making the dependency update workflow proactive we reduce queue contention and accelerate dependency hygiene.

What this changes (high level):
- CI: introduce three explicit shard profiles (fast/unit, medium/integration, slow/e2e) and a lightweight profile selector that picks a shard subset based on changed paths and heuristics.
- CI: add path-aware test runner so lint and unit tests only run for affected packages when the change is scoped.
- CI: add cache-rotation policy (TTL + LRU hint) to avoid stale docker layer re-use across node-version bumps.
- Automation: add a dependency-triage bot (scripts/dep-triage/) which runs weekly, scans package manifests (go.mod, package.json, pyproject.toml), and opens candidate PRs with owners + labels (deps/minor, deps/patch) for non-breaking upgrades. The bot integrates with our internal Linear tags and a lightweight risk scoring to avoid noisy upgrades for transitive major bumps.
- Tooling: introduce a slim builder image and split Dockerfile into build/runtime stages to shave ~220MB off the CI image and reduce upload time to the runner cache.

Checklist: 
- [x] Implement shard profiles and selector
- [x] Implement path-aware test gating
- [x] Add cache rotation and TTL for docker/build caches
- [x] Add dep-triage bot and basic scoring
- [x] Update CI docs and add troubleshooting notes
- [x] Manual dogfood run on the staging pipeline

Commits (summary):
- feat(ci): add test-profile selector and profile definitions
- feat(ci): implement path-aware test gating for unit/lint runs
- perf(ci): introduce cache-rotation TTL and LRU hinting for runner caches
- feat(automation): add dep-triage bot scaffolding and weekly workflow
- refactor(docker): split Dockerfile into slim builder + runtime
- docs(ci): update CONTRIBUTING.md and ci/README with new shard guidance

Files changed (high level):
- .github/workflows/ci.yml (rewritten to call profile selector and conditional jobs)
- .github/workflows/dep-triage.yml (new weekly workflow for bot)
- ci/profile-selector.py (new helper used by CI)
- ci/path-gate.py (path-based gating logic)
- scripts/dep-triage/* (bot code, templates, scoring rules)
- images/ci/Dockerfile (split into multi-stage)
- docs/ci/README.md (update)
- CONTRIBUTING.md (ci iteration section)

Small snippet examples included in PR: a condensed selector rule used by the workflow: paths matching src/python/ or sdk/python/ trigger "fast+medium" profile; changes to infra/ or e2e/ trigger slow profile. The path-gate implements a cheap git-diff parse and a file-type map to map changed files to test suites.

Review conversation (high level):
- Ethan Park (reviewer): "Nice decomposition. Two requests: 1) add metrics for profile selection decisions so we can audit why a shard was picked; 2) make the dep-triage PR body include an opt-out label so platform owners can pause automation per package."
- Maya Chen (author): "Added telemetry events (ci.profile.selected reason tag) and added the opt-out label mechanism (deps/automation-paused) to the bot. See commit feat(ci): telemetry + dep-triage optout in second push."
- Rhea Kapoor: "Please add a smoke job that runs the medium shard against the staging cluster on a cron to catch regressions we may miss when only unit tests run."
- Maya Chen: "Added a staging-smoke job to .github/workflows/ci.yml; it runs nightly and is gated by a flag in ci/config.yaml."
- Samir Patel: "Security scan step should still run for dependency bot PRs — we can't auto-merge without a security check."
- Maya Chen: "Dep-triage opens PRs as draft and CI runs the security-scan job; the merge automation waits for a maintainer to approve after checks pass. Documented in the workflow comments."
- Approval summary: Ethan approved after telemetry added; Rhea approved after adding staging smoke; Samir approved after explicit security gating added.

CI run history on this PR:
- Initial push (2026-02-18): integration runner timed out on one slow e2e test (flaky infra teardown). job rerun was scheduled.
- Second push (2026-02-20): fixed a minor timing issue in e2e teardown, triggered full CI; fast and medium shards passed; slow shard had a single flaky test which was quarantined (skipped with ticket ENG-8270).
- Final push (2026-02-22): all shards green, security-scan passed, dep-scan reported two low-severity transitive issues (documented in dep-triage output).

CI checks summary: build: pass; unit-tests (fast): pass; integration (medium): pass; e2e (slow): pass (after quarantine); lint: pass; security-scan: pass; dep-scan: pass; docker-build: pass; cache-priming: pass.

Performance impact measured in dogfood runs: median CI runtime for changes touching single packages decreased from ~24m to ~9m when path-gating correctly selected the fast profile. Full-suite runtimes for cross-cutting changes are unchanged but now run on dedicated slow runners to avoid head-of-line blocking. Runner queue wait times dropped ~37% during a 48h experiment window.

Rollout plan:
- Step 1: merge to main behind a feature flag; enable path-aware selection for a subset of repos (sdk-* and examples) for 48h.
- Step 2: expand to monorepo packages incrementally and monitor ci.profile.selected metrics and fail rates for 7 days.
- Step 3: enable dep-triage bot in draft mode org-wide (no auto-merge) and collect maintainer feedback.

Post-merge notes and follow-ups:
- OWNERS mapping needs a small update for several legacy packages (tracked in ENG-8288).
- Add alerting for the staging-smoke job failing more than twice in 24h (follow up ENG-8299).
- Consider adding an opt-in auto-merge path for patch bumps after 48h and successful security passes (future work).

Merge: This PR was rebased and merged to main on 2026-02-23 after all reviewers approved and CI was green.

Related: ENG-8123 (CI scalability), ENG-8270 (e2e flaky teardown quarantine), ENG-8288 (owners cleanup).

Notes for release: include a bullet in the next platform changelog about faster CI for scoped changes and the dependency triage bot (non-breaking, targeted automation to improve dependency hygiene).

title: Invoke compatibility proto mapper and decision telemetry
preview: Motivation: Customers expect OpenAI-style function/tool calling to 'just work' when routed through Redwood's serving runtime. However, in practice we see a set of recurring issues: heterogeneous tool descriptors from third-party models, ambiguous tool_choice semantics when multiple tools match, and stream fragmentation that splits the tool invocation payload across SSE boundaries. This PR introduces a compatibility-focused proto mapper and decision telemetry that together make tool invocation more deterministic and observable.Design summary: 1) proto_mapper: a lightweight rule engine that canonicalizes incoming OpenAI-compatible invocation envelopes into a single Redwood internal representation. It normalizes fields (name/description/schema/arguments), expands legacy aliases into canonical redwood_tool_id, and attaches a compat_hint when the mapping required heuristics. Mappings are deterministic and extensible via a small YAML ruleset shipped with the package. 2) choice_scorer: when multiple tools match a requested function name/schema, the scorer computes an ordered candidate list using schema affinity (how well the provided arguments conform to each tool schema), stream-priming context (tokens already generated that imply certain tool semantics), and operational priors (recent latency/cost observed for the customer's tenant). The winner receives an explicit confidence score and a short rationale encoded in metadata. 3) stream_coordinator: improves streaming stability by aligning fragments to logical tool frames: partial JSON fragments are buffered and resynchronized rather than forwarded to downstream tool adapters; we emit a fragment_resync event for observability. 4) telemetry: emit an invoke.decision event for every tool invocation that contains candidate list, scores, chosen_index, confidence, request_id, and quick pointers to the proto_mapper rule that fired. This event is low-cardinality and intended for debugging regressions and production investigations.Changes and files: See changed_files for a compact list. Key behavioral changes are limited and guarded behind a feature flag 'openai_compat_proto_mapper' that defaults to on for Hosted only and off for Dedicated/Private until customers opt in. Tests: added unit tests covering nested JSON arguments, missing schema resolution, alias expansion, and integration tests that simulate streaming SSE fragmentation across multiple boundaries to verify deterministic reassembly and correct candidate scoring. Performance: choice_scorer is designed to be cheap (fast schema affinity checks and cached token-based signals). Benchmarks on a 4k sequence workload show median decision latency ~= 0.9ms additional per-invoke at p50 and 2.4ms at p95 under load (detailed numbers in benchmark artifact attached to CI). Rollout and risk mitigation: rollout is staged. Hosted -> 10%/50%/100% over two weeks with monitoring dashboards. Dedicated/Private customers will see release notes and can enable via console or config. We included a migration script to generate alias mappings for large customers that rely on legacy tool names.Open questions and future work: - Consider surfacing the decision rationale in debug traces for customers with private deployments. - Expand rule engine to accept safe machine-suggested mappings (requires guardrails). - Add optional delta compression for repeated identical invocation envelopes to reduce egress cost.Review conversation snapshot: See review_thread field for representative comments. Merge strategy: squash; small logical commits squashed to a single logical change and merged to main.Approvals: Approved by core runtime reviewers and SRE, CI passed and manual integration harness validated in staging.

title: Introduce latency-distribution-collector and histogram-exporter for perf runs
preview: Motivation: our current perf suite reports mean/median p50/p95/p99 but lacks a first-class histogram distribution export that lets ops and product slices examine tail behavior by bucket and request class. This PR introduces a latency-distribution-collector (benchmarks/runner/latency_collector.py) and a histogram_exporter that emits fixed-window histograms and a Prometheus-compatible scrape endpoint for aggregated CI runs. Key changes: 1) adds a thread-safe latency_collector that supports labeled buckets (model, route, sequence_length_bin) and controlled flush intervals; 2) implements histogram_exporter with configurable bucket boundaries and JSON/Prometheus output modes; 3) integrates collection step into ci/perf-pipeline.yml so nightly jobs persist artifacts to the perf artifacts store; 4) adds validation tests and an examples script to reproduce locally. Implementation notes: the collector uses a ring buffer to bound memory and an optimized bucket increment path to minimize overhead during tight throughput tests; the exporter supports on-the-fly delta exports to reduce payload size for long runs. Checklist: - [x] design and prototype latency collector; - [x] unit tests covering concurrency and bucket edge cases; - [x] exporter with both JSON and Prometheus formats; - [x] CI integration and artifact upload; - [x] docs and example runbook. Backwards compatibility: output formats default to JSON with the old summary generator still available; no changes to existing benchmark input schemas. Perf: measured <1% overhead at 2k rps in synthetic harness; added ability to capture per-route tail behavior that was previously invisible. How to validate: run examples/bench/collect_and_export.sh against a local runner to produce artifacts and then run docs/benchmarks/latency-distribution.md validation steps which include checksum and bucket-sanity assertions. title: evidence-pack-assembler-and-runbook-heuristics
preview:
Summary: This change adds an "evidence pack" assembler to the incident-bot pipeline and a set of runbook heuristics to automatically curate the most relevant diagnostics for an alert. The assembler collects contextual artifacts (metrics snapshots, recent logs, trace spans, KV cache summaries, and SLO delta snapshots), ranks them using lightweight heuristics, prunes/pivots sensitive fields, and generates a compressed evidence bundle that can be attached to runbooks and pager notifications.

Motivation and context: On long-running incidents engineers spend a lot of time pulling the same set of data from multiple systems for triage. This PR is targeted at reducing mean time to acknowledgement and the handoff friction by producing a focused, size-bounded artifact that the oncall and responder can consume in the first 10 minutes. It also standardizes what we include for downstream postmortems and automated RCA stitches. Related tickets: ENG-4521 (runbook automation), SRE-78 (oncall evidence standard).

Design: 
- Evidence assembler service (in-process to incident-bot) with pluggable collectors for: metrics snapshots, recent logs (time-window based with token caps), traces (top N spans by error rate), config diffs, and a short KV cache dump summary.
- Heuristics engine prioritizes artifacts by signal-to-noise: anomaly score (from SLO-delta), error-class frequency, recent deploy detection, and cross-service call error propagation. We keep the heuristics intentionally interpretable (weighted rules + decay factors) to facilitate debugging and tuning.
- Privacy & size constraints: All artifacts are subject to field scrubbing rules using the existing redaction registry; large payloads are truncated or replaced with a sampling summary. Evidence packs are capped at 2MB by default and stored encrypted (KMS-tagged) for 7 days; retention and KMS key are configurable via RBACed console controls.
- Integration points: The assembler runs during alert enrichment (pre-notification), can be requested ad-hoc from the incident console, and is attached to runbook drafts created by the incident-bot. A feature flag gated rollout allows opt-in by team.

Implementation highlights: 
- new internal/curation/assembler.go: core orchestration, assembler API, size-aware bundling.
- new internal/curation/heuristics.go: compact rule engine and default rule set with unit tests.
- pkg/runbooks/generator.ts: runbook templates extended to include a signed link to evidence pack and human-readable summary.
- ops/playbooks/templates/evidence-default.yaml: configuration schema exposed to ops.
- docs/runbooks/evidence-pack.md: usage, troubleshooting, and security considerations.

Testing & CI: Unit tests for the heuristics rules and scrubber added; an e2e workflow (.github/workflows/evidence-pack.yml) runs a smoke assembly against fixture data and validates size/encryption headers. I intentionally included simulated noisy alerts to assert the assembler prunes correctly. CI passed after a single rerun where a flaky external test (trace fixture) was adjusted.

Rollout plan: staged by team flag. Phase 1: enable for SRE and a small set of platform teams (2 weeks) to capture feedback and tweak heuristics. Phase 2: broader opt-in and metrics collection (evidence pack attach rate, median size, time-to-first-artifact). Fallback: if assembler fails or triggers size/security validation, incident-bot attaches a short diagnostic summary and logs the error for postmortem.

Checklist: 
- [x] Core assembler implemented + unit tests
- [x] Heuristics rule set + tests
- [x] Redaction and size enforcement integrated
- [x] Docs + ops config added
- [x] CI e2e workflow added
- [x] Feature-flag + staged rollout plan

Risks and mitigation: The main risk is overfitting heuristics to a narrow set of incidents and leaking sensitive fields. Mitigations: conservative default rules, rigorous redaction by registry, short retention, and a configurable opt-out.

Notes for reviewers: Focus on the rule definitions in heuristics.go (they are intentionally simple) and the scrubber integration in assembler.go. I included a couple of example evidence packs under ci/test/fixture/evidence.json for reference.

If merged, follow-up work tracked under ENG-4613 (adaptive heuristics tuning) and SRE-90 (evidence retention policy extension).

title: add-prefetch-latency-matrix-and-alert-accordion
preview: Context: We've observed recurring small latency spikes during prefetch/warmup warm paths that do not trigger existing SLO alerts because they are below coarse aggregation thresholds. Motivation: Provide finer-grained visibility into prefetch-phase latency and introduce an alert 'accordion' that gracefully widens or narrows a route's alert sensitivity based on correlated signals (kv-cache hit rate, kernel-queue depth, and tenant priority). What this PR does: 1) Adds a Prefetch Latency Matrix metric pipeline (histogram shards per 50ms slab) to capture warmup latency per invocation phase and per-tenant micro-slice; 2) Adds a dashboard JSON for the matrix with cohorting controls (route, model, quantization profile, tenant tier) and interactive sparkline overlays for kernel queue and KV occupancy; 3) Implements an Alert Accordion policy in alerts/accordion_policy.go that composes multi-signal thresholds and uses a weighted dampening window (configurable) to escalate or de-escalate alert sensitivity; 4) Exposes a compact invocation log snippet sampler that attaches a short context payload when a prefetch slab crosses a threshold for easier triage; 5) Adds docs and runbook entries for interpreting matrix cohorts and for tuning accordion parameters. Rollout plan: feature-flag gated for 3 days in staging, then canary to 10% of production traffic in us-east-1, monitoring false-positive rate for 48h, then full rollout. Backwards compatibility: Metrics and API are additive; existing alerts are untouched by default. Testing: unit tests for accordion logic and e2e replay harness using synthetic warmup traces (replaybench); load test shows 2.8% p95 reduction in unnoticed prefetch spikes vs baseline while producing <0.5% additional alert volume when tuned to default thresholds. Related: ENG-2334 outlines the experimental detection logic, PM-801 tracks dashboard UX. Merge notes: squash-merge; no schema migration required.

title: Circuit stabilizer: connection quieting and tenant SLO-tilt hotpatch
preview: Motivation: During an incident on 2026-02-14 we observed rapid circuit reopen/reclose oscillations for certain islanded regions which amplified load and caused tenant-level SLO violations. Root cause analysis showed two compounding issues: (1) the circuit reconciler could race when reconciling restored health probes and reopen attempts, and (2) the shedder decision logic treated all SLO deficits equally, causing aggressive shedding for tenants where small latency increases were acceptable and for others where impact was severe. This PR introduces a targeted hotpatch that (a) adds a short 'connection quieting' window after a circuit state change to prevent immediate reopen retries, (b) introduces a tenant SLO-tilt score that biases the regional shedder away from high-impact tenants when feasible, and (c) fixes the reconcilation race by serializing state transitions through a single reconcile queue per-region. The change is intentionally conservative: default quiet window is 3s and tilt weight defaults to 0.2 to avoid large policy swings. Changes: - core/slo/tilt.go: new tilt scoring util and config hooks that expose a per-tenant weight used by the shedder. - serving/circuit/reconciler.go: serialized region reconcile queue plus idempotent transition checks to prevent flip-flopping. - serving/circuit/quieting.go: small in-memory LRU that tracks recent transitions and prevents reopen attempts until quiet window expires. - serving/shedder/region_decision.go: incorporate tilt score into weighted shedding decision; maintain preferred fallback model selection when high-tilt tenants are present. - telemetry/events.go: emit QuietWindowStart/QuietWindowEnd and TiltScoreComputed events. - integration/e2e/quieting_test.go: deterministic test that simulates probe flapping and asserts no oscillatory reopen. Backwards compatibility and rollout: defaults preserve current behavior for customers who do not tune tilt or quieting via runtime config. Feature is behind a runtime flag `enable_connection_quieting` (true in this hotfix rollout). We will canary the change to the US-west region for 24h before global rollout. Observability: added two new histograms (quiet_window_duration, tilt_score) and two counters for quieting hits/misses. Telemetry tags include tenant_id and region. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for tilt scoring and reconcile queue - [x] Integration e2e that reproduces the incident pattern - [x] Telemetry emits validated in CI - [x] Manual canary in us-west for 24h - [x] Documentation note in CHANGELOG and runtime flags section Risks and mitigations: The quiet window could increase recovery latency for true healthy nodes; mitigated by keeping default low (3s) and exposing config for operators. Tilt weighting could under-shed a tenant that contributes to global load — the weight is advisory and the shedder still enforces capacity caps. Rollback plan: revert runtime flag or roll back the deployment; reconciler changes are backward-compatible because they are serialized but do not change transition semantics. Contact: SRE on-call + Priya for triage.

title: adaptive-capacity-harmonizer-and-fallback-inspector-hotfix
preview: Motivation: We observed a multi-region incident where aggressive rejoin behavior and aggressive failback caused repeated oscillations of traffic between regions. This triggered SLO thrashing and higher tail latency for customers routed through small-capacity regions. This hotfix introduces two coordinated mechanisms: an adaptive capacity harmonizer (server-side quota reservoir + per-region rejoin damping) and a sticky fallback inspector (diagnostics + short-term sticky fallback policy) to prevent rapid oscillation during regional recovery windows. Summary of changes: implement quota reservoir to reserve a tiny emergency budget when a region reports healthy but not yet warmed KV caches; add rejoin damping (cooldown fence + exponential rejoin backoff) to the node lifecycle; expose fallback-inspector endpoint for on-call debugging and live decisions; wire new metrics (fallback.harmonizer.*, fallback.inspector.*) and add tracing spans for decision points; add unit/integration tests, a small docs incident writeup, and a canary rollout config. This PR is a targeted hotfix intended to stabilize regional failover behavior while we work on a longer-term scheduler rewrite. The change is runtime-only and non-breaking for customer APIs. See linked Linear ticket ENG-4218 for design notes and postmortem rationale.

title: introduce kernel-variance prober to map per-layer sequence-length inefficiencies
preview: Motivation: We frequently see tail latency regressions that only appear for particular sequence-length distributions. Existing workload runners produce aggregate token-level metrics but do not attribute variance to model kernels or layers. This PR adds a 'kernel-variance prober' that runs focused microbenchmarks across sequence length windows and collects fine-grained per-kernel timing and variance profiles so we can prioritize kernel/graph-level optimizations and targeted quantization tradeoffs. Change summary: - New binary tools/bench/prober/kernel_variance_prober.py: orchestrates probing campaigns across sequence-length buckets, random seeds, and batch shapes; supports warmup/steady-state detection and adaptive sampling. - Benchmark harness extensions bench/harness/sequence_sampler.py: sequence-length aware samplers and an API to plug sampling policies into existing runners. - Profiling exporter bench/reporters/kernel_variance_reporter.go: aggregates raw kernel timing logs into per-layer heatmaps and high-variance segment reports; outputs ndjson for downstream ingestion. - Integration test bench/tests/test_kernel_variance_endtoend.py: small runtime smoke verifying consistent profiling across CPU/GPU backends. - CI job .github/workflows/benchmark-prober.yml: a lightweight workflow that runs the prober in a constrained emulation mode for PRs to prevent regression in core loops. Why this is different: instead of only measuring latency or throughput aggregates, the prober attributes variance to kernel implementations (attention, rotary, layernorm, matmul) and to sequence-length regions which helps routing, batching and kernel-selection decisions in the serving runtime. Usage notes: the prober can be invoked locally with `tools/prober/run_prober --profile-config examples/probes/length_scan.yaml`; output is an ndjson stream consumable by the reporting pipeline. Backwards compatibility: no runtime or serving changes; this is a benchmark-only change. Rollout plan: land as experimental tooling behind a feature flag; run nightly full-resolution campaigns for critical models and surface regressions in the benchmark dashboard.

title: introduce-lightweight-local-dev-runner-and-toolchain-stub
preview:
Context and motivation: onboarding and fast feedback for local CI iterations has regressed as our CI matrix grew. This PR introduces a lightweight local dev runner (tools/fastdev) and a minimal toolchain stub (dev/Toolchain.toml) plus a small workflow that developers can run locally or in ephemeral self-hosted runners to get the same sanity checks as CI without the full matrix. This is an opt-in developer convenience feature that reduces time-to-first-green and iteration time on lint/test changes. Summary of changes: - add tools/fastdev/runner.go: small Go binary that runs a subset of checks in-process with conservative timeouts and filesystem caching - add tools/fastdev/README.md: usage, examples, and how to wire into VS Code tasks - add dev/Toolchain.toml: lightweight list of pinned tool versions for local dev (go, node, python, prettier, husky minimal config) - add .github/workflows/dev-local.yaml: workflow targeted at self-hosted/dev-runner label and usable as reference for local runs - add .pre-commit-config.yaml entries and Makefile target fastdev - add scripts/ci/sanity-check.sh to share common checks between CI and fastdev Why not full devcontainer? Full devcontainers are heavier and we want a quick path for contributors who already have the environment. The Toolchain.toml is intentionally minimal and human-editable; it is not authoritative for production. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (ENG-4172) [x] unit tests for runner logic [x] small e2e smoke used locally by author [x] docs and README for opt-in usage Implementation notes: the runner currently shells out to the language toolchain where appropriate but uses a caching layer (tools/fastdev/.cache) to avoid repeated installs during rapid iterations. It ships a dry-run mode and a --ci-compat flag which performs the same commands as the CI sanity lane (lint, go vet, pytest -k sanity). Small illustrative snippet of the workflow (from .github/workflows/dev-local.yaml):
name: Dev local referenced
on: [workflow_dispatch]
jobs:
  fastdev:
    runs-on: self-hosted
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./tools/fastdev/runner --ci-compat

Changes are conservative: no production CI lane behaviors removed; existing CI still the source of truth. Rollout plan: merge to main and advertise opt-in in the contributor guide; monitor adoption and iterate. Performance expectations: local fastdev runs should be ~30-60s for small changes on modern dev machines, substantially faster than full CI matrix. Risks and mitigations: - risk: divergence between fastdev and CI -> mitigation: include --ci-compat mode that runs identical commands, and we add a CI check that runs the fastdev dry-run in a nightly job to detect drift. - risk: contributors rely on the stub as authoritative -> mitigation: call out in README that Toolchain.toml is a local convenience. Related PRs & notes: this PR intentionally avoids changing release automation and focuses on developer feedback loops; follow-ups will propose integrating a thin prebuild cache and a remote runner for fast-path checks.

Risks and mitigations: - risk: divergence between fastdev and CI -> mitigation: include --ci-compat mode that runs identical commands, and we add a CI check that runs the fastdev dry-run in a nightly job to detect drift. - risk: contributors rely on the stub as authoritative -> mitigation: call out in README that Toolchain.toml is a local convenience. Related PRs & notes: this PR intentionally avoids changing release automation and focuses on developer feedback loops; follow-ups will propose integrating a thin prebuild cache and a remote runner for fast-path checks.

title: Attenuated warm-pool smoothing and utilization exposer for dedicated fleets
preview: Context: Dedicated fleet warm pools are currently susceptible to short-lived microbursts: a handful of tenants generate transient spikes that cause the warm-pool controller to over-allocate headroom, inflate cost, and produce noisy per-tenant utilization metrics. This PR introduces an attenuated smoothing policy for warm-pool sizing and a lightweight utilization exposer that decouples instantaneous spikes from the long-running reservation logic. Motivation: reduce oscillation-driven overprovisioning, improve stability for tenant SLAs, and provide clearer telemetry to platform teams so they can tune reservations and burst contracts. Changes in this PR (high level): - Introduce an attenuated smoothing window with configurable decay factors and min/max headroom bounds. The algorithm applies exponential decay to short spikes while allowing sustained demand to grow warm pool capacity. - Add microburst detector that can tag allocations as transient (microburst) vs sustained; microburst signals are retained short-term and influence only ephemeral warm allocations. - Expose per-tenant utilization summaries (P50/P95/P99 token throughput and burst incidence rate) to telemetry pipeline with a compact protobuf payload for ingestion. - Split warm-pool decision path: "elastic" (longer smoothing) vs "ephemeral" (microburst buffer) to keep committed reservations stable. - Add feature flag and runtime knobs: warm_pool.smoothing.enabled, warm_pool.smoothing.decay_tau_ms, warm_pool.ephemeral_budget_pct, telemetry.util_bucket_window_ms. Checklist: - [x] Core algorithm and controller changes implemented - [x] Unit tests for smoothing kernel and microburst tagging - [x] Integration test that simulates mixed sustained + bursty tenants and validates headroom change bounds - [x] Telemetry schema and exporter implementation - [x] Docs: operator guide and runtime knobs - [x] Feature-flag gating for staged rollout Commits (summary): - init: add smoothing kernel and interfaces (pkg/autoscaler/smoothing.go) - controller: wire smoothing into warm-pool reconciler and split decision path (pkg/autoscaler/controller.go) - telemetry: add utilization exposer and protobuf (internal/metrics/utilization_exposer.go, proto/utilization.proto) - exporter: push compact metrics to metrics pipeline with sampling (cmd/dedicated/telemetry/exporter.go) - tests: unit and integration harness (tests/integration/warm_pool_smoothing_test.go) - docs: operational guide and examples (docs/operational/warm-pool.md) Files changed (high-level): pkg/autoscaler/smoothing.go, pkg/autoscaler/controller.go, internal/metrics/utilization_exposer.go, proto/utilization.proto, cmd/dedicated/telemetry/exporter.go, docs/operational/warm-pool.md Why this is safe: - Backwards compatible: smoothing is behind warm_pool.smoothing.enabled (default=false for initial rollout). - Telemetry schema is additive; consumers that don't parse the new proto will ignore the new field. - Behavior can be reverted by toggling feature flag or adjusting decay_tau_ms to a neutral value. Testing notes: - Local integration harness simulates 100 tenants with 10% producing 2s microbursts and confirms <7% warm-pool capacity change compared to baseline. - Unit tests cover edge cases including zero traffic, sustained ramp, and pathological bursts. Review conversation summary: - Ethan: requested clearer bounds on min/max headroom and unit for decay_tau_ms. I added validation and docs explaining units are milliseconds and provided reasonable defaults (decay_tau_ms=5000ms, min_headroom=1, max_headroom=5). - Miguel: asked for an additional metric to report number of microburst-tagged allocations per minute; added microburst_alloc_count and unit test. - Ava: suggested moving the telemetry exporter to a separate goroutine to avoid blocking reconciler; refactored exporter to use buffered channel and background worker and added backpressure handling. CI / checks: - CI run 1: go vet + unit tests (flaky DB connection in test harness) -> failed; reran. - CI run 2: full unit + integration (short) -> pass. Observability: - dashboards: added warm-pool smoothing dashboard panel (docs/operational/warm-pool.md) showing headroom, microburst rate, and utilization percentiles. - Alerts: recommend adding alert warm_pool.unexpected_growth if headroom increases >50% over 5m window. Migration/rollout plan: - Stage 1 (dev): enable smoothing for dev clusters for 48h; monitor microburst_alloc_count and headroom trend. - Stage 2 (staging): enable for 1 week, bounded by feature-flag autoswitch if warm_pool.unexpected_growth fires. - Stage 3 (canary->prod): gradual ramp to 10% fleets, then 30%, with automated rollback criteria. Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main after approvals and green CI. Merge commit: abc123def4567890. Release notes: Added attenuated warm-pool smoothing to reduce reactive overprovisioning from short-lived tenant microbursts; introduced per-tenant utilization exposer (P50/P95/P99 + microburst incidence); feature flag warm_pool.smoothing.enabled controls rollout. Followups / TODOs: - ENG-6012: surface per-tenant utilization in Console route. - ENG-5687: tune default decay_tau_ms via A/B tests across dev fleets.

title: Cost Model API: add policy guardrails for what-if projections (bounds + allowed ranges)
preview: Context: The new Cost Model what-if endpoint is intentionally flexible (batching/caching/quantization knobs) and will be called by Optimize (interactive) and potentially Console (hot paths). In early staging tests and after SUP-31877 triage, we saw two failure modes: (1) callers passing extreme parameter values (e.g., batch size 4096, cache warm-rate > 1.0) leading to unrealistic savings outputs, and (2) expensive computations when callers submit high-cardinality workload summaries (large histograms / too many route keys) that can blow our latency budget and increase tail p95s. This PR adds explicit policy guardrails at the API boundary so the service is safe-by-default, deterministic, and bounded. Summary of changes: (A) Introduce a centralized PolicyGuardrails layer for what-if requests that validates and clamps input ranges (where clamping is explicitly allowed) and rejects invalid/unsafe requests with clear error codes. (B) Enforce request bounds for cardinality: max number of workload segments, max histogram buckets, max distinct route/model keys, and max scenario count per request. (C) Add a compute budget heuristic: estimate worst-case complexity from the request and reject if above threshold (protects CPU and avoids unbounded fanout to model catalog/telemetry lookups). (D) Add an allowlist for parameterized what-if dimensions in v1 (batching, prefix-cache, kv-cache, quant profile change) and explicitly reject unknown/experimental dimensions unless the caller is in an internal allowlist (feature flag + service-to-service identity). (E) Standardize error responses for guardrail failures: HTTP 400 with error_code=CM_GUARDRAIL_* for caller-correctable issues; HTTP 403 for forbidden dimensions/tenancy; HTTP 429 for rate-limit triggers; and a consistent message + remediation hints so Optimize can surface actionable UI text. Guardrails (v1): - scenario_count: max 10 scenarios per request; - workload_segments: max 25 segments (Optimize currently uses <= 8); - histogram buckets: max 32 buckets for seq_len distribution and max 32 for output_len distribution; - route/model keys: max 50 distinct keys; - batch_size_target: [1, 256] (reject outside); - microbatch_size_target: [1, 64]; - cache_hit_rate: [0.0, 1.0] (reject outside; no clamping); - cache_warm_rate: [0.0, 1.0] (reject outside; note: renamed internally to warm_rate to avoid ambiguity); - quantization_profile_id: must exist in model catalog and must be compatible with base model; - overhead_allocation_policy: must be one of {request_attributable_only, blended_cluster_overhead}; - max_estimated_compute_units: 1.0 (internal scale); reject above threshold. Observability: adds metrics cost_model.whatif.guardrail_reject_total{reason=...} and cost_model.whatif.request_shape{segments=...,buckets=...,scenarios=...} to support tuning. Also adds structured log fields guardrail_reason and request_shape_summary for incident triage. Tests: unit tests for validation + error mapping; golden tests ensuring deterministic output and stable error codes; load-test fixture ensuring guardrails prevent worst-case request shapes from entering compute. Notes: This PR intentionally rejects requests rather than silently clamping in most cases, to avoid producing misleading ROI estimates. The only clamping performed is for missing optional fields that have safe defaults (e.g., absent cache_hit_rate defaults to observed baseline). Follow-ups: (1) coordinate with Optimize to align UI input widgets with these ranges and surface error_code messages; (2) document guardrails in internal docs; (3) revisit limits after we collect two weeks of production telemetry on request shapes and latency.

title: Introduce telemetry-driven synthetic load generator and hyperops for batch profile autotuning
preview: Problem: Production telemetry indicates wide variance in per-customer request shape (seq lengths, prompt/templates, temperature) which makes static batching defaults suboptimal. We need a way to synthesize representative load from existing traces, run lightweight microbenchmark passes, and generate conservative batch/cache/quantization profiles that are safe for rollout. This PR implements a telemetry-driven 'synthetic-econ-proxy' tool plus runtime hyperops that drive an autotuner. Goals: 1) generate reproducible synthetic streams from sampled traces, 2) run a fast hyperparameter sweep for batch sizes, microbatch windows, and cache TTLs in a sandbox mode, 3) attach a compact 'handoff' metadata blob to candidate profiles for safe staged rollout and automatic rollback. Key features: - synthetic-econ-proxy CLI: consumes sampled telemetry (p50/p95 seq distribution, client headers, model family) and emits a deterministic request stream for local or staged perf runs. - hyperops runner: lightweight orchestrator that runs short trials across candidate batch/microbatch/kv-cache policies and collects cost/latency metrics. - profile handoff format: single-line compact metadata (versioned tag, checksum, compatibility hints, suggested fallback) used by the runtime to decide compatible rollouts. - safety: candidate profiles are generated with conservative SLO buffers and include automatic fallback to prior profile on SLA regressions. - developer UX: preview command (redwoodctl perf preview --profile <file>) that replays the synthetic stream against a chosen model variant and prints unit economics estimates. Motivation and context: This is an engineering-first approach to reduce human iteration when choosing defaults for new customers or workloads. Rather than relying on a single scoring heuristic, we synthesize a workload closest to the customer's telemetry and run a controlled sweep to find economically sensible defaults. See linked Linear items and test harness in the commits. Checklist: - [x] synthetic stream generator - [x] hyperops runner and metric collectors - [x] compact profile handoff format and compatibility checks - [x] redwoodctl preview command + CLI docs - [x] unit and integration tests - [x] e2e smoke runs on staging - [x] changelog/release notes Comments: This PR purposefully keeps quantization changes limited to attaching quant 'hints' to profiles rather than forcing conversion; quantization conversions remain an explicit operator action. The approach avoids large runtime changes and hooks into existing rollout machinery.

title: per-request graceful-degradation: kernel compatibility shim + precision-hint API
preview:
Motivation: We continue to see tail latency regressions in mixed-precision kernels when models with large kv-cache footprints are routed through the 'fast' execution path. This PR introduces a lightweight compatibility shim and a per-request 'precision hint' header that lets the router/scheduler choose a conservative execution mode for requests that prioritize correctness over absolute token throughput.

What this PR does (single-string description): Adds a kernel-compat shim layer in the runtime that detects binary incompatibility or quantization sensitivity for a model+request combination and exposes a low-overhead boolean that the scheduler uses to avoid aggressive fusion/quantization fast-paths; implements an opt-in HTTP header (X-Redwood-Precision-Hint: prefer-correct | prefer-fast | neutral) and SDK flags that map to the same hint; extends continuous-batching logic to respect per-request hints by routing hinted requests to a separate lightweight queue that favors conservative kernels and smaller batch coalescing; introduces a KV-cache safety check that prevents prefix-optimized eviction heuristics from running for hinted requests; updates the metrics to surface how many requests used the compatibility shim and the delta in p50/p95 latency and token cost; includes tests and performance microbenchmarks.

Design notes and trade-offs: The shim is intentionally minimal: it does not change model weights or quantize on the fly. Instead it flips kernel-selection heuristics (no aggressive fused attention kernels, prefer fp16-safe paths, disable some prefetch transforms) and reduces batch coalescing windows to reduce peak memory pressure on hinted requests. This increases per-token cost but avoids correctness edge-cases introduced by certain fused kernels (observed in edge-case tests with malformed KV cache shapes). We chose a header + SDK-first API because it is the least invasive while enabling customers and platform teams to opt-in per-call.

Migration and rollout: The feature is behind a server-side flag (runtime.enable_precision_hint_shim). Default is off for all clusters; enabled in staging during canary; default for some internal teams with model reg tests. Rollout plan attached in the checklist.

Checklist: - [x] shim implementation and unit tests - [x] SDK header plumbing (python + ts) - [x] scheduler queue change and e2e tests - [x] add metrics and dashboard panels - [x] perf microbenchmarks (attached) - [x] rollout gating flag and docs

Related: Linear: ENG-4280, ENG-4297. Incident: SUP-1223 (customer reported hallucination/regression after aggressive kernel upgrade). More context: see internal doc https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4280/precision-hint-design (internal link).

Notes for reviewers: focus on runtime/shim correctness (no changed behavior for default-off), scheduler behavior (deadlocks or starvation between two queues), and SDK ergonomics. The PR intentionally avoids changing model catalog metadata until we validate metrics from canary.

Release notes: Adds an opt-in per-request precision hint header and a runtime compatibility shim to favor correctness over fused-kernel throughput.

title: Add rolling resilience-curve canary with tiered fallback and rate fallthrough
preview: Motivation: We observed slow, multi-hour regressions that did not trigger existing spike-focused detectors; this PR adds a time-weighted 'resilience curve' canary that measures cumulative degradation slope across cohorts and triggers tiered safeguards. High level: 1) rolling resilience curve evaluator that computes a decay-weighted degradation area under curve (AUC) for latency and error rate across multiple cohort slices (region, client-class, route). 2) temporal quorum voting: requires consensus from multiple independent cohorts before a hard fallback. 3) tiered fallback policy: soft fallback (traffic shaping + alert only), model-variant fallback (route to a lower-cost stable variant), and rate fallthrough (graceful request shedding with progressive SLAs). 4) adaptive probe scheduling that increases probe frequency for cohorts showing rising AUC and backs off when stabilized. Checklist: - [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-18721) - [x] unit + integration tests - [x] e2e smoke in perf lab - [x] docs added under docs/perf - [x] fallthrough behavior validated with canary simulator Implementation notes: The resilience curve is implemented in canary/engine/resilience_curve.py with an efficient incremental aggregator and configurable half-life. Voting is implemented as a quorum module that accepts weighted cohort votes. Fallbacks are implemented in alerts/rollout_guard.go and routing/soft_fallback.rs. This PR also adds a canary simulator harness and two smoke tests. Related: builds on prior probe-scheduling work (ENG-17234). Risk/rollback: feature gated behind canary.v2 flag; rollout guard defaults to soft mode until explicitly enabled in the console. Testing: see test/integration/resilience_smoke_test.py and perf-canary lab run id perf-lab-20260212-07. Merge notes: squash-merged to main after addressing review comments and CI fixes.

title: Triad SDK credential simplifier and stream-flow parity (ergonomics & retry harmonization)
preview:
Motivation: SDK consumers across Python/TypeScript/Go reported friction when configuring credential refresh, retry presets, and stream cancellation semantics. This PR introduces a compact, consistent credential surface and harmonizes retry profiles + stream flow semantics across all three SDKs so application code can be ported with minimal changes.

Summary of changes: consolidated 'CredentialProvider' API shape, introduced a lightweight 'CredHint' for prefetching, aligned retry defaults (idempotent vs. non-idempotent) and added a small stream-control API that exposes cancelable checkpoints for long-running pull-streams. The change is intentionally additive: it builds atop existing adapters and provides compatibility shims for older consumer code.

title: Deferred consensus rollout adapter with heuristic recovery
preview:
Motivation: Production rollouts that commit traffic swaps as soon as the control plane reports target replicas healthy still expose end users to transient regressions from cold KV cache, session affinity mismatches, or slow tail latency on a subset of cohorts. This change introduces a 'deferred consensus' adapter that requires affirmative acknowledgements from a configurable quorum of session-cohorts before finalizing a model swap, plus a heuristic-driven recovery path that can pause, degrade or automatically roll back a swap when post-swap signals indicate regression.

What this PR does (high level): Adds a new rollout adapter that sits in the orchestration plane and coordinates multi-cohort commit semantics: 1) cohort-level readiness probes (application + KV cache warm status + sample latency quantiles) 2) a consensus window where cohorts must report stable metrics for N consecutive intervals 3) an optional TTL fallback to avoid stalls 4) a heuristic recovery engine that applies graded responses (traffic hold, selective bleedback, or immediate rollback) when composite signals exceed thresholds. Also implements a lightweight KV-cache snapshot and prefetch handshake used during rollback to reduce cold-start noise.

Key user-visible behavior changes: Teams can opt into 'deferred_consensus' policy in rollout specs. When enabled, rollouts will not finalize until the configured quorum returns positive readiness within the consensus window. On failures the adapter will publish a named rollback reason and attach an anonymized snapshot fingerprint to the rollout event to help debugging (no raw model data or inputs are included).

Concrete changes in this PR: - orchestration/adapter/deferred_consensus.go: new adapter implementation + controller glue - orchestration/api/rollout_schema.yaml: new policy fields and validation - agent/cohort_probe: probe implementation that returns readiness + kv-snapshot-hash - runtime/kvcache/snapshot.go: compact snapshot + prefetch protocol (gRPC) - console/rollouts: added per-session trace link and rollout event details - tests: unit tests for consensus arithmetic, integration test stubbing 3 cohorts, e2e with simulated latency spikes - docs: rollout policy reference and migration notes

Migration notes and backward compatibility: Default behavior remains unchanged. The new policy is opt-in. For customers using session-affinity heavy workloads we recommend enabling the policy behind a canary rule with a single cohort before wider adoption. KV snapshotting requires the runtime to expose the prefetch gRPC endpoint; the change is backward compatible (adapter falls back to existing warmup behavior if endpoint not present).

Testing and validation: Local unit tests (100% for adapter core), simulated integration matrix with 3 cohorts on perf-canary harness, staged dogfood run on Dedicated cluster for 48 hours. The perf-canary run injected controlled tail-latency regressions to validate graded recovery actions. Metrics observed: reduced false-positive rollbacks by 34% vs immediate-swap baseline; rollback downtime reduced by median 23s due to snapshot prefetch during failover.

Rollout plan: merge -> enable behind feature-flag (redwood.rollout.deferred_consensus) -> enable for internal dogfood as 1-cohort canary on 2026-02-19 -> 7-day telemetry evaluation -> gradual expansion. Rollback window for first canary: 2 hours with automated alerting to oncall SRE.

Security & privacy: snapshot fingerprints contain only compact hashes and runtime metadata; no customer tokens or model parameters are serialized. All control-plane events are audited via existing audit-log exporter pipeline.

title: ack-window-coalescing-and-reconnect-probe-for-resumable-streams
preview:
Context: We have had intermittent production incidents where client disconnect followed by rapid reconnects created duplicate tool/function calls or left server-side inflight work stranded. This PR introduces a lightweight client ACK window and a reconnect probe handshake to make streaming sessions more resilient to flapping connections while preserving OpenAI compatibility for client-driven cancels.

Goals:
- Allow the client to acknowledge receipt of terminal tokens (finalized structured output / function call invocation) so the server can safely drop inflight state sooner and avoid double-execution on reconnects.
- Coalesce rapid client cancel/soft-abort signals within a short ACK window to reduce noisy cancellation churn during flaky networks.
- Add a reconnect probe (small idempotency-aware handshake) that validates resume tokens and server lease state before resuming expensive work (tool calls/KV-cache continuations).
- Maintain compatibility with current OpenAI-style cancel/close semantics — no change required for most clients.

What changed (high-level):
- Protocol: add optional "ack_window_ms" header on initial stream request (default 3000ms). During the ack window the server accepts a single cancel token and will delay aggressive resource reclamation for that window; repeated cancels within the window are treated as coalesced (no new cancel events emitted to tool call layer).
- Protocol: introduce a short probe exchange during reconnect: client sends a lightweight probe with last-seen sequence id and resume_token; server responds with probe_ack and explicit lease_state (active/draining/expired). Resume proceeds only on probe_ack==ok and lease_state==active (or draining with explicit allow_resume flag).
- Server: tracking of client-ack receipts for final structured outputs. When a client ACK for a terminal event is received, the server will mark the session checkpointed and allow immediate resource reclamation without waiting for full TTL. This avoids duplicate tool invocations on quick reconnects.
- Tool/fn calling: calls are now annotated with an idempotency fingerprint (derived from call_id + resume_seq) and the runtime consults a short-lived dedupe cache keyed by that fingerprint. The cache lifetime is aligned to the ack_window + server_drain_timeout to balance dedup protection with memory usage.
- SDKs/compat: no breaking API change for typical streaming use. New optional headers are supported by Redwood's OpenAI-compat adapter; we added transparent fallbacks for clients that don't send the new fields.

Implementation notes:
- Added stream_ack.go in internal/runtime to centralize ack/coalescing logic and metrics.
- Modified openai/compat adapter to parse ack_window_ms and to run the reconnect probe flow on connection resumptions.
- Added a short-lived in-memory dedupe cache with metrics and an adaptive eviction timer. For Dedicated/Private, cache can be backed by local ephemeral Redis if configured (opt-in).

Testing and validation:
- Unit tests for ack coalescing, probe handshake, and dedupe cache eviction.
- Integration test that simulates client disconnect/reconnect flapping and asserts no duplicate tool calls and that structured output is only emitted once.
- Load test scenario: 10k streams with randomized disconnect windows to measure overhead; results show <=0.6% p99 token latency increase and 18% reduction in duplicate tool calls under flapping scenarios.

Checklist:
- [x] Design doc linked (ENG-912)
- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Backwards compatible adapter changes
- [x] Metrics/instrumentation (stream.ack.* and stream.probe.*)
- [x] E2E flapping load test results attached

Rollout plan:
- Merge behind feature flag stream_ack_coalesce (off by default).
- Enable for 10% of hosted traffic for 24h; monitor stream.probe.failures and tool.duplicate.detected.
- Enable SDK opt-in examples in quickstarts after 10% ramp is healthy.

Notes/Tradeoffs:
- Acknowledgement window delays aggressive server TTL reclamation; chosen default (3s) is conservative — short enough to limit resource ties, long enough to absorb network blips.
- Dedup cache increases memory pressure slightly; adaptive eviction mitigates long tails. For Dedicated customers we provide config knobs to tune ext cache.

Related/precedent: several internal incident postmortems (SUP-428) drove this work. This complements prior resumable-handshake PRs but focuses on coalescing/cancellation semantics and an explicit probe phase to gate resume onto server lease health.

title: model-catalog: add filters, compatibility flags, and pricing/limits fields to /models API
preview: Summary: Adds richer filtering and capability metadata to the public model catalog endpoints. This PR introduces query filters for capability-based discovery and model attributes, plus explicit compatibility flags and first-class pricing/limits fields on model descriptors. Motivation: customers have asked for a machine-readable way to pick models by capability (e.g., tool-calling, structured output, streaming-stable) and to programmatically surface cost/limit information for routing and billing. The current /models response is sparse and requires ad-hoc parsing of descriptions in downstream services. What this changes: 1) API surface: GET /v1/models now accepts new query params: 'capability' (comma-separated values: chat, completion, embedding, tool_calling, structured_output, streaming), 'openai_compat' (true|false), 'pricing_tier' (free|standard|premium), and 'min_max_tokens' (min,max). Example: GET /v1/models?capability=tool_calling,structured_output&openai_compat=true 2) Response schema additions: each model object gains optional fields: 'capabilities' (string[]), 'compatibility_flags' (string[]; e.g. "openai_chat", "openai_completion", "openai_embeddings"), 'pricing' (object with flat_rate, per_token_input_usd, per_token_output_usd, currency), and 'limits' (object: max_tokens, max_requests_per_minute, concurrent_requests). These are optional to preserve backwards compatibility; existing clients will continue to work if they ignore new fields. 3) OpenAI compatibility adapter: adapter maps compatibility_flags to legacy OpenAI-style fields for the OpenAI compatibility layer. Added translation layer so openai_compat=true returns aliases expected by older clients. 4) Server-side indexing: index added for capability tags to keep catalog query latency within SLOs (see perf notes below). 5) Docs & SDK: OpenAPI spec updated, python/ts SDKs surface the new fields and query helpers. Compatibility/migration notes: fields are additive and optional; no required fields were changed. The only risk is downstream code that relies on the previous string parsing of model descriptions — we've added an integration test and a migration note in docs. Rollout plan: staged rollout with feature flag 'model_catalog_caps_v1'; default enabled for beta accounts and disabled for strict compat customers. Monitoring: new metrics added: model_catalog.filter_latency_ms, model_catalog.cache_hit_rate, and pricing_field_populated_ratio. Related: Linear ENG-1729 (catalog filter work), PM-404 (pricing requirements). Checklist: - [x] API schema and OpenAPI docs updated - [x] Server implementation of filters and response enrichment - [x] OpenAI compatibility adapter translation layer - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] SDK updates (python + ts) - [x] Perf benchmark added to benchmark-lab - [x] Docs + release notes Notes: see inline snippets below for example response and query. Example response snippet (illustrative): {"id":"rw-mini-1","name":"rw-mini","capabilities":["chat","embedding"],"compatibility_flags":["openai_chat","openai_embeddings"],"pricing":{"per_token_input_usd":"0.00001","per_token_output_usd":"0.00002","currency":"USD"},"limits":{"max_tokens":"4096","max_requests_per_minute":"600","concurrent_requests":"10"}}
